EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As steady precipitation associated with a warm front moved back into Sussex County on the evening of the 29th, temperatures were still around freezing over some of the higher terrain. It was still warm enough aloft for precipitation to fall as rain. At the ground it froze on contact on some exposed surfaces, roadways and bridges and made for slippery travel on untreated surfaces. As southeast to south winds continued through the night, temperatures rose above freezing.

A band of precipitation associated with a warm front moved across New Jersey during the late morning and early afternoon of the 29th. While it was warm enough above the ground for precipitation to fall as rain, temperatures at the surface did not have enough of a chance to recover to above freezing after a clear start to the night. Pockets of light freezing rain or sleet occurred, especially in valley locations. Ice accretions were less than one twentieth of an inch. Untreated roadways, bridges and overpasses were slippery. Farther to the east and southeast, temperatures rose above freezing before the precipitation arrived.

A strong south pressure gradient between a strong offshore high pressure system and an approaching cold front caused south winds to gust from 45 to 55 mph.  Strong winds downed trees and power lines across parts of Northeast New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 55 mph was measured at the West New York weather station at 5:28 pm EDT.  Numerous trees and wires were downed at 238 York Avenue in Jersey City at 4:49 pm EDT.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 53 mph was measured by the Fort Lee weather station at 4:38 pm EDT.  High winds downed numerous trees and power lines in Fort Lee.  Power lines were downed at 23 Daniel Court in Ridgewood.  Trees and wires were downed on 66 East Crescent Avenue in Ramsey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain fell across Sussex County during the day on the 26th. Precipitation started as light snow in the morning. As warmer air moved in aloft, it changed to sleet and freezing rain during the first half of the afternoon and to plain rain by the second half of the afternoon. Snow accumulations were less than an inch and ice accretions were less than one-tenth of an inch. Untreated roadways were slippery.||The wintry mix was caused by a low pressure system that moved from the Central Plains on the afternoon of the 25th, through the Ohio Valley on the night of the 25th, and across the southern tier of Pennsylvania and central New Jersey during the afternoon and evening of the 26th.

Strong south winds during the morning of February 13th forced the collapse of two large window walls at the Ocean Township Elementary School gymnasium, which scattered glass. About 30 to 40 students from two gym classes were in the room when the window gave way, however none were injured.

The combination of the onshore circulation caused by a high pressure system to the north and a low pressure system passing to the south and spring tides associated with the full moon caused minor tidal flooding during the morning high tides on the 22nd and 23rd in Monmouth County.  At Sandy Hook, the high tide reached 7.23 feet above mean lower low water on the morning of the 22nd and 7.15 feet above mean lower low water on the morning of the 23rd. Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.70 feet above mean lower low water. ||The coastal flooding was caused by a low pressure system that formed along the Gulf Coast States during the afternoon of the 21st. This low pressure system moved northeast and reached Mississippi on the morning of the 22nd and the Tennessee Valley during the early afternoon of the 22nd. A secondary low pressure formed along the South Carolina coast that morning. The secondary low pressure system continued to move northeast and by 7 p.m. EST on the 22nd was located about 125 miles east of Long Beach Island, New Jersey. It passed just south of Nova Scotia overnight. The surface high pressure system that preceded the low to the north contributed to the onshore flow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation caused by a warm front approaching the region started as light sleet and light freezing rain over the northwest corner of Sussex County on the morning of the 31st. Temperaures rose above freezing by late that morning and all of the precipitation changed to plain rain.

The runoff from a prolonged period of rain that started on the evening of the 18th and continued into the early morning of the 20th caused minor flooding along sections of the Millstone River in Somerset County. Event totals averaged between 1 and 2 inches. The Millstone River at Griggstown was above its 10 foot flood stage from 830 a.m. EDT on the 20th through 1245 a.m. EDT on the 21st. It crested at 10.38 feet at 1130 a.m. EDT on the 21st. Event totals included 1.60 inches in Belle Mead and Skillman, 1.58 inches in Somerville, 1.54 inches in Pottersville and 1.44 inches in Blackwells Mills. The prolonged rain was caused by a low pressure system that moved from the central Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 18th, into the Ohio Valley on the morning of the 19th before weakening. Another low pressure system formed on its cold front and was in the lower Tennessee Valley on the morning of the 19th. It moved northeast and passed just north of the area overnight on the 19th.

A cold front was preceded and accompanied by strong to severe thunderstorms across northwest New Jersey on the evening of the 1st. Some pea size hail was also reported with the thunderstorms in Hope (Warren County) and Butler (Morris County). The air mass preceding the front was an unseasonably warm and humid one for early April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down some trees and wires in Blairstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system over the Ohio and Tennessee Valleys Tuesday afternoon, February 12, lifted northeast into the lower Great Lakes region early Wednesday morning, February 13. As the occluded front approached the east coast Wednesday afternoon, a secondary low developed along the triple point over southern New Jersey, then tracked northeast and off the southern New England coast in the evening. Light to moderate snow broke out across the region Tuesday afternoon. As the cold air eroded with the primary low tracking to the west, the precipitation transitioned to freezing rain during the early morning hours Wednesday. Prior to the changeover, 2 to 4 inches of snow fell, followed by 1/4 to 1/2 inch of ice. Temperatures rose above freezing in the mid to late morning hours with a transition to plain rain.

Low pressure over the Lower Mississippi Valley Thursday night, January 31, lifted Northeast and into the Ohio Valley by Friday afternoon, February 1. Light to moderate freezing rain broke out across the Lower Hudson Valley and Northeast New Jersey Friday morning ahead of a warm front over the Middle Atlantic states. The precipitation went over to rain from south to north by late afternoon, leaving about half an inch of ice across Western Passic County in Northeast New Jersey.
Thunderstorms that moved through southwest New Jersey early in the morning on the 23rd caused lightning damage in Salem County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike and the ensuing fire destroyed the steeple of the First Assembly of God Church on Georgetown Road in Carneys Point Township. The fire was starting to spread to the main structure before it was contained.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire continued to burn 3200 acres of woodland on the Fort Dix Military Base just on the Ocean County side of the base in early September. The fire started in a containment area of the base and was first reported on August 28th. It remained in a remote firing range area of the base. As a precaution live fire exercises were halted in the area. The fire never threatened any buildings on the base and natural fire breaks prevented it from spreading to those areas. The fire burned out at 130 p.m. EDT on September 3rd. Fort Dix firefighters as well as fire fighters from New Egypt and Cookstone battled the blaze.

A father and son were dragged farther offshore by a rip current on a Long Beach Township Beach. The pair made it about halfway to the shore by the time the beach patrol arrived and helped them out of the water.

Strong rip currents on September 7th claimed the life of a 38-year-old man and led to multiple rescues along Monmouth County beaches that day. A 38-year-old male was caught in a rip current off the Newark Avenue Beach in Spring Lake during the afternoon. He was pulled from the surf at the Washington Avenue Beach and died in the hospital later that day. Lifeguards and volunteers from four communities responded. In Long Branch, a police officer rescued a 23-year-old man caught in a rip current off the Pier Village jetty. Both suffered cuts and bruises. In Sea Bright, a 48-year-old man lost consciousness in the ocean and was rescued by other beachgoers and hospitalized. In Bradley Beach, two people were rescued from the ocean. The enhanced or high risk of rip currents in the wake of Tropical Storm Hanna which passed through the state on the evening of the 6th.

A wintry precipitation mix affected the higher terrain of New Jersey from the morning of November 30th into the morning of December 1st. Less than an inch of snow accumulated and then around one-third of an inch of ice accrued on exposed surfaces before precipitation changed to rain. In the valleys, precipitation quickly changed from snow to rain. ||Precipitation started as snow around 8 a.m. EST on November 30th and changed to freezing rain by around 10 a.m. EST on November 30th over the higher terrain and plain rain in the valleys. Light freezing rain continued through the day and evening before enough warm air moved in at the surface to change the freezing rain to plain rain during the early morning of December 1st. Untreated roadways and walkways were hazardous.||The wintry precipitation was caused by a low pressure system that developed in the western Gulf Coast states on the 29th. It moved northeast into the Ohio Valley on the morning of the 30th and into extreme southwest Ontario Province on the morning of December 1st. A high pressure system located in New England as precipitation started supplied enough cold air near the surface to be channeled into the county to cause precipitation to fall as freezing rain. The precipitation ended when the low pressure system's occluded front moved through the county prior to sunrise on December 1st.

A strong cold front helped trigger showers and thunderstorms across the central and southern New Jersey during the late afternoon and early evening on the 15th. Thunderstorm winds capsized a boat in Gloucester County and a severe shower caused wind damage in Ocean County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 23-year-old man was presumed dead after the boat he was in capsized as a line of showers and thunderstorms moved through the county. He was one of four persons on board who were duck hunting on the Mantua Creek near the Interstate 295 bridge near Paulsboro when the boat overturned. The other three (a 12-year-old boy, a 24-year-old man and a 41-year-old man) managed to cling to the boat and then reach a sandbar from which they were saved about an hour later. The peak wind gust at nearby Philadelphia International as the line moved through was 36 mph. ||Tides were higher than normal because of the combination of the strong up the Delaware Bay and Delaware River wind flow and the spring astronomical tides associated with the recent full moon. The early afternoon high tide at Reedy Island (New Castle County Delaware) reached 7.50 feet above mean lower low water. Minor tidal flooding starts at 7.20 feet above mean lower low water. The mid afternoon high tide in Philadelphia reached 8.61 feet above mean lower low water. Minor tidal flooding starts at 8.20 feet above mean lower low water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent gale force northwest flow down Delaware Bay caused blow-out tides to occur during the early morning low tide along upper parts of Delaware Bay on January 3rd. Low tide at Reedy Island (New Castle County) reached -1.88 feet below mean lower low water. Blowout tides start at -1.80 feet below mean lower low water.

A nor'easter that developed off the Carolina coast on the night of the 4th caused a pounding surf and beach erosion along the New Jersey Coast on the 5th and 6th. It also claimed the life of a man in Monmouth County. At about 11 a.m. EST on the 5th, a man who was fishing on a jetty in Avon, slipped into the ocean. He was rescued about ten minutes later, but could not be saved. The nor'easter formed off the Carolina coast overnight on the 4th and slowly moved northeast. It was located near Cape Hatteras, North Carolina at 7 a.m. EST on the 5th and off of southern Delmarva Peninsula at 7 a.m. EST on the 6th. It then slowly weakened as it moved farther offshore the next two days.

Strong south winds preceded the arrival of a squall line of showers and thunderstorms and a cold frontal passage during the mid day hours on the 15th. In addition to knocking down some weak tree limbs and wires, the combination of the winds and spring tides associated with the recent full moon caused some minor tidal flooding at the time of the late morning high tide on the 15th. The high tide forced the closure of New Jersey State Route 47 over the Grass Sound Channel into Wildwood (Cape May County). The high tide reached 7.53 feet above mean lower low water in Cape May (Cape May County) and about 7.30 feet above mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 7.7 feet above mean lower low water.

Skies cleared quickly after light rain fell during the afternoon and early evening on the 22nd. Because winds were also light, black ice formed on some area roadways overnight. In Burlington County, a 48-year-old man died after his Sports Utility Vehicle skidded off of Creek Road in Moorestown Township and struck a tree. In Tabernacle Township, a 30-year-old woman was injured after her Sports Utility Vehicle hit an icy patch, went off the road, struck a tree, returned to the road where it collided with another vehicle and then overturned.
A slow moving cold front that crossed the Tri-State on the 15th, produced some severe thunderstorms across portions of Northeast New Jersey during the afternoon and early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike produced major house damage at 20 North Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain affected most of New Jersey on the 11th and 12th and caused poor drainage, field and river flooding. The subsequent river and stream flooding started on the 11th and persisted through the 15th in Morris County. Rain began during the afternoon of the 10th and continued through the 11th into the early morning on the 12th. Rain fell at its heaviest from the afternoon of the 11th until just after Midnight EST on the 12th. Event precipitation totals average three to five inches. ||The heavy rain and flooding were caused by a low pressure system that developed along the Gulf Coast States on the 10th. This intensifying low pressure system moved northeast from Alabama on the morning of the 11th, to central North Carolina early in the evening on the 11th to near Bridgeport, Connecticut at 7 a.m. EST on the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streets in Hanover Township were flooded.||The main stem of the  Passaic River at Pine Brook  was above its 19 foot flood stage from 900 a.m. EST on the 13th through 915 p.m. EST on the 15th. It crested at 19.41 feet at 430 a.m. EST on the 14th. The Rockaway River above the Boonton Reservoir was above its 5 foot flood stage from 854 a.m. EST on the 12th  through 1230 a.m. EST on the 13th. It crested at 5.42 feet at 215 p.m. EST on the 12th. The Rockaway River below the Boonton Reservoir was above its 5 foot flood stage from 1045 a.m. EST on the 12th  through 707 p.m. EST on the 13th. It crested at 6.11 feet at 9 p.m. EST on the 12th. The East Ditch at Beaver Brook Road was above its 3.9 foot flood stage from 420 a.m. EST through 459 p.m. EST on the 12th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow fell across southeastern New Jersey during the afternoon of the 24th. Accumulations averaged 1 to 3 inches. The fast movement offshore kept accumulations down and also prevented snow from spreading farther north deeper into New Jersey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds affected southeast New Jersey from late in the evening on the 11th into the early morning of the 12th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph. Schools in Atlantic County in both Estell Manor and Weymouth were closed because of power outages. Peak wind gust included 47 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport.||The strong south winds were caused by an intensifying low pressure system that developed along the Gulf Coast States on the 10th. This low pressure system moved northeast from Alabama on the morning of the 11th, to central North Carolina early in the evening on the 11th to near Bridgeport, Connecticut at 7 a.m. EST on the 12th. The strongest winds occurred when the region was in the warm sector of the low pressure system. Stronger winds aloft were able to mix down more efficiently to the surface. The strong winds ended after the cold front moved through the area early on the 12th.

An ice storm affected the higher terrain of Sussex County from the evening of the 10th into the morning of the 12th. Up to one inch of ice accrued on exposed surfaces and knocked down numerous tree limbs, trees and power lines. Hardest hit was the Highland Lakes region of Vernon Township, but ice damage also occurred in Sandyston, Sparta, Walpack, Wantage, and Montague Townships. Ice damage started at elevations greater than 1000 feet. Because of the recent warm weather, most of the ice accretions occurred on trees and other exposed surfaces and not as much on roadways.  About 5,000 homes and businesses lost power. Power was not fully restored until the 13th.||In Vernon Township, in Highland Lakes almost every road was blocked by downed trees. Trees fell for about a 24 hour period starting early on the 12th. Electric companies had to cut trees off roadways both getting to the repair and then again exiting the region. Poles were snapping by the weight of the ice itself. An emergency shelter was opened. Schools were closed on the 12th. Other parts of Vernon Township hart hit included Barry Lakes, Lake Conway, Lake Wanda and Lake Panorama. Downed trees tore down ski lifts in Hidden Valley. |||Precipitation started as rain early on the 10th. After a cold front moved through the area on the 10th, colder air started to move into the region. Temperatures slowly dropped and spent most of the rest of the event right around the freezing mark from late in the evening on the 10th until it ended on the morning of the 12th. Precipitation briefly changed to sleet and snow before it ended on the morning of the 12th. ||The ice storm was caused by a combination of a high pressure system that moved across nearby Canada that supplied fresh cold air near the surface and a low pressure system that developed along the Gulf Coast States on the 10th. This low pressure system moved northeast from Alabama on the morning of the 11th to near Bridgeport, Connecticut at 7 a.m. EST on the 12th.

Strong to high winds buffeted New Jersey from the late morning through the evening on the 31st. Hardest hit by the wind were the extreme southern part of the state as well as Sussex County. The highest wind gusts occurred during the afternoon of the 31st, except in Sussex where the highest wind gusts occurred during the evening. Numerous tree limbs, trees and power lines were knocked down. The majority of the 13,000 customers losing power were in the southern part of the state.||The most serious reported injury occurred in Elsinboro Township in Salem County. A 59-year-old woman broke her neck after a downed tree fell onto her vehicle. Elsewhere in the county, in Pennsville a downed tree damaged a home. About 1,100 homes and businesses lost power mainly in Elsinboro, Mannington and Carneys Point. In Atlantic County, a downed tree badly damaged a trailer in the Atlantic Garden Complex in Egg Harbor Township. About 2,000 homes and businesses lost power in the county. In Cumberland County, a large pine tree fell onto a moving vehicle in Vineland. The occupants were not hurt. About 1,000 homes and businesses in the county lost power. In Gloucester County, a downed tree damaged the sunroom of a home in Glassboro. About 3,300 homes and businesses lost power. ||Peak wind gusts included 75 mph in Pennsville (Salem County), 68 mph in Brigantine (Atlantic County), 65 mph at High Point (Sussex County), 63 mph in Wantage (Sussex County) and Cape May (Cape May County), 54 mph at Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), Atlantic City International Airport and Millville (Cumberland County), 51 mph in Point Pleasant (Ocean County) and 46 mph in Trenton (Mercer County).||The high winds were caused by the large pressure difference between a rapidly intensifying Alberta Clipper type low pressure system moving through New England and a strong high pressure system approaching from the upper Mississippi Valley.

In Atlantic County, a downed tree badly damaged a trailer in the Atlantic Garden Complex in Egg Harbor Township. About 2,000 homes and businesses lost power in the county. In Cumberland County, a large pine tree fell onto a moving vehicle in Vineland. The occupants were not hurt. About 1,000 homes and businesses in the county lost power. In Gloucester County, a downed tree damaged the sunroom of a home in Glassboro. About 3,300 homes and businesses lost power. ||Peak wind gusts included 75 mph in Pennsville (Salem County), 68 mph in Brigantine (Atlantic County), 65 mph at High Point (Sussex County), 63 mph in Wantage (Sussex County) and Cape May (Cape May County), 54 mph at Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), Atlantic City International Airport and Millville (Cumberland County), 51 mph in Point Pleasant (Ocean County) and 46 mph in Trenton (Mercer County).||The high winds were caused by the large pressure difference between a rapidly intensifying Alberta Clipper type low pressure system moving through New England and a strong high pressure system approaching from the upper Mississippi Valley.

Heavy rain affected most of New Jersey on the 11th and 12th and caused poor drainage, field and river flooding. The subsequent river and stream flooding started on the 11th and persisted through the 15th in Morris County. Rain began during the afternoon of the 10th and continued through the 11th into the early morning on the 12th. Rain fell at its heaviest from the afternoon of the 11th until just after Midnight EST on the 12th. Event precipitation totals average three to five inches. ||The heavy rain and flooding were caused by a low pressure system that developed along the Gulf Coast States on the 10th. This intensifying low pressure system moved northeast from Alabama on the morning of the 11th, to central North Carolina early in the evening on the 11th to near Bridgeport, Connecticut at 7 a.m. EST on the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to the heavy rain, there was also minor tidal flooding at the time of the morning high tide along the ocean front on the 12th.  The morning high tide at the Cape May Ferry Terminal reached 6.94 feet above mean lower low water. Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water.||Event precipitation totals included 5.27 inches in West Cape May, 4.89 inches in Wildwood, 4.81 inches in Erma and 4.30 inches in Woodbine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm affected most of the northern half of New Jersey from  the morning of the 19th into the early part of the 20th. A wintry mix fell across the Raritan Basin and the rest of central New Jersey and precipitation fell more and more as snow across the northern third of New Jersey. Snow and sleet accumulations averaged 1 to 4 inches across the Raritan Basin and 5 to 9 inches in northwest New Jersey. Ice accretions averaged around one-quarter of an inch in the Raritan Basin.||Precipitation started on the morning of the 19th as rain and snow across northern New Jersey. As the intensity increased, precipitation changed back to snow. Only in Mercer did precipitation stay as sleet or freezing rain. As warmer air moved in aloft, precipitation changed to all freezing rain and sleet in the Raritan Basin during the afternoon of the 19th. Precipitation remained mainly snow in places farther to the north. In Middlesex, Monmouth and Mercer Counties, surface temperatures rose above freezing by late in the afternoon of the 19th and remained above freezing until the rain ended that evening. Elsewhere, temperatures remained below freezing until the event ended. Precipitation ended around Midnight EST in the Raritan Basin and during the early morning of the 20th in northwestern New Jersey. ||The wintry weather led to many accidents. In Mercer County, two police officers and a prisoner were injured during a head-on collision in Hopewell Township. Vehicles were overturned on New Jersey State Route 29 in Ewing and on Interstate 295 in Lawrence and Hamilton. In Morris County, northbound Interstate 287 was backed up for three miles. The southbound side was bumper to bumper the entire length of the county. Dozens of minor accidents were reported throughout the county. In Warren County, about 30 crashes were reported, some vehicles veered off roads. ||Some schools in northern New Jersey never opened. Others postponed after school sporting events and activities. Some smaller businesses closed. Non-emergency state workers were dismissed at 3 p.m. EST. In Morris County, county offices and Superior Court closed during the early afternoon. New Jersey Transit and Light Rail honored bus passes, but ridership overall was cut in half.||Specific accumulations included 9.6 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 8.0 inches in Newton (Sussex County), 7.5 inches in Butler (Morris County), 6.6 inches in Blairstown (Warren County), 6.4 inches in Marcella (Morris County), 6.0 inches in Hackettstown (Warren County), 4.5 inches in Parsippany (Morris County), 4.0 inches in South Plainfield (Middlesex County), 3.4 inches in Stewartsville (Warren County), 3.0 inches in Bridgewater (Somerset County), 2.9 inches in New Brunswick (Middlesex County), 2.5 inches in Whitehouse (Hunterdon County) and 1.5 inches in Manalapan (Monmouth County). ||The winter storm was caused by a low pressure system that moved from the central Rockies on the morning of the 18th east to Illinois on the morning of the 19th. The low pressure system reached the western Pennsylvania border early in the afternoon on the 19th. A secondary low pressure system then formed on its warm front in Delmarva and by early in the evening on the 19th became the main low pressure system. At that time it was already racing quickly east of the New Jersey coast. Of equal importance was a high pressure system that shadowed the low as it moved across the southern tier of Canada. It provided a fresh supply of cold air and assisted in having more precipitation fall as snow.

Strong to high winds buffeted New Jersey from the late morning through the evening on the 31st. Hardest hit by the wind were the extreme southern part of the state as well as Sussex County. The highest wind gusts occurred during the afternoon of the 31st, except in Sussex where the highest wind gusts occurred during the evening. Numerous tree limbs, trees and power lines were knocked down. The majority of the 13,000 customers losing power were in the southern part of the state.||The most serious reported injury occurred in Elsinboro Township in Salem County. A 59-year-old woman broke her neck after a downed tree fell onto her vehicle. Elsewhere in the county, in Pennsville a downed tree damaged a home. About 1,100 homes and businesses lost power mainly in Elsinboro, Mannington and Carneys Point. In Atlantic County, a downed tree badly damaged a trailer in the Atlantic Garden Complex in Egg Harbor Township. About 2,000 homes and businesses lost power in the county. In Cumberland County, a large pine tree fell onto a moving vehicle in Vineland. The occupants were not hurt. About 1,000 homes and businesses in the county lost power. In Gloucester County, a downed tree damaged the sunroom of a home in Glassboro. About 3,300 homes and businesses lost power. ||Peak wind gusts included 75 mph in Pennsville (Salem County), 68 mph in Brigantine (Atlantic County), 65 mph at High Point (Sussex County), 63 mph in Wantage (Sussex County) and Cape May (Cape May County), 54 mph at Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), Atlantic City International Airport and Millville (Cumberland County), 51 mph in Point Pleasant (Ocean County) and 46 mph in Trenton (Mercer County).||The high winds were caused by the large pressure difference between a rapidly intensifying Alberta Clipper type low pressure system moving through New England and a strong high pressure system approaching from the upper Mississippi Valley.

Snow fell across southeastern New Jersey during the afternoon of the 24th. Accumulations averaged 1 to 3 inches. Most of the accumulation occurred within a two hour period as a narrow band of heavy snow affected southern parts of Ocean County and central Atlantic County. Temperatures dropped below freezing when the snow started and untreated roads were slippery. Many accidents were reported.||Specific accumulations included 3.5 inches in Beach Haven (Ocean County), 2.5 inches at the Atlantic City International (Atlantic County) and Eldora (Cape May County), 2.3 inches in Absecon (Atlantic County), 2.0 inches in Ship Bottom (Ocean County), Woodbine (Cape May County) and Mays Landing (Atlantic County), 1.5 inches in Leesburg (Cumberland County) and Cape May City (Cape May County) and 1.3 inches in Estell Manor (Atlantic County).   ||The snow was caused by a low pressure system that formed over the North Carolina coastal waters on the morning of the 24th. The low pressure system moved quickly eastnortheast and by 7 pm EST on the 24th was already about 325 miles east of Fenwick Island, Delaware. The fast movement offshore kept accumulations down and also prevented snow from spreading farther north deeper into New Jersey.

Heavy rain affected most of New Jersey on the 11th and 12th and caused poor drainage, field and river flooding. The subsequent river and stream flooding started on the 11th and persisted through the 15th in Morris County. Rain began during the afternoon of the 10th and continued through the 11th into the early morning on the 12th. Rain fell at its heaviest from the afternoon of the 11th until just after Midnight EST on the 12th. Event precipitation totals average three to five inches. ||The heavy rain and flooding were caused by a low pressure system that developed along the Gulf Coast States on the 10th. This intensifying low pressure system moved northeast from Alabama on the morning of the 11th, to central North Carolina early in the evening on the 11th to near Bridgeport, Connecticut at 7 a.m. EST on the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Event precipitation totals included 3.68 inches in Swedesboro, 3.46 inches in Franklinville and 3.20 inches in West Deptford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty west to northwest winds occurred during the late morning and afternoon on the 7th across New Jersey. The strong winds helped fuel a fire that badly damaged a home in Monroe Township in Gloucester County. Peak wind gusts averaged 45 to 50 mph and included 55 mph in High Point (Sussex County), 51 mph at the Cape May Ferry Terminal (Cape May County), 50 mph at the Atlantic City Marina (Atlantic County), 47 mph in Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), 46 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport, 45 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), 44 mph in Trenton (Mercer County) and 43 mph in Millvlle (Cumberland County). The strong winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system that was moving through the Saint Lawrence Valley and a high pressure system building east from the central Mississippi Valley.

Some intermittent light freezing rain fell across the higher terrain of Sussex County from late in the evening on the 26th into the early morning of the 27th. Ice accretions were less than one tenth of an inch. Untreated roadways though were slippery. The light precipitation was caused by a warm front that was bringing warmer air into the region from the central Appalachians and Ohio Valley.

A wintry mix of precipitation affected northwest New Jersey as well as the Raritan Basin in central New Jersey from the afternoon and early evening of the 16th into the morning of the 17th. In spite of nearly twenty-four hours of precipitation, the intensity was mainly light. About 1 to 3 inches of snow accumulated before precipitation changed to freezing rain with ice accretions that averaged around two-tenths of an inch. ||Precipitation started as snow during the late afternoon of the 16th in the Raritan Basin and spread north into Sussex County by early evening. As warmer air moved in aloft, the snow changed over to freezing rain during the late evening of the 16th and continued overnight. Precipitation changed over to plain rain just as it was ending in the Raritan Basin, but ended as freezing rain in places farther to the north around sunrise on the 17th.  ||The wintry mix caused numerous accidents and made traveling home difficult during the evening commute. Vehicles slid off roads, into each other and into ditches. About 15 accidents alone were reported in and around Sayreville (Middlesex County) on the Garden State Parkway. After school classes, sports activities and concerts were cancelled in Hunterdon, Somerset and Middlesex Counties. ||Actual snow accumulations included 4.2 inches on Hell Mountain in Hunterdon County, 3.0 inches in Whitehouse (Hunterdon County), 2.8 inches in Morris Township and Roxbury Township (Morris County), 2.5 inches in Independence Township (Warren County), 2.4 inches in North Brunswick (Middlesex County), 2.2 inches in Franklin Township (Somerset County), 2.0 inches in Hopewell (Mercer County), Stewartsville (Warren County), Marcella (Morris County) and Bridgewater Township (Somerset County) and 1.0 inch in Holmdel (Monmouth County) and Sparta Township (Sussex County)  ||The wintry mix of precipitation was caused by a low pressure system that developed on a cold front in the Gulf Coast States on the morning of the 16th. It moved northnortheast and reached the Tennessee Valley early in the evening on the 16th and northeast Ohio at 1 a.m. EST on the 17th. The low pressure system then moved into the Great Lakes around sunrise on the 17th as a secondary low pressure system was forming off the New Jersey coast. The secondary low slowly intensified as it moved toward Cape Cod on the 17th as the primary low weakened over the Great Lakes. An arctic high pressure system moved across New York and New England on the 16th and provided a fresh supply of cold air near the surface that prevented temperatures from rising above freezing during this event across much of central and northwest New Jersey.

Strong to high winds buffeted New Jersey from the late morning through the evening on the 31st. Hardest hit by the wind were the extreme southern part of the state as well as Sussex County. The highest wind gusts occurred during the afternoon of the 31st, except in Sussex where the highest wind gusts occurred during the evening. Numerous tree limbs, trees and power lines were knocked down. The majority of the 13,000 customers losing power were in the southern part of the state.||The most serious reported injury occurred in Elsinboro Township in Salem County. A 59-year-old woman broke her neck after a downed tree fell onto her vehicle. Elsewhere in the county, in Pennsville a downed tree damaged a home. About 1,100 homes and businesses lost power mainly in Elsinboro, Mannington and Carneys Point. In Atlantic County, a downed tree badly damaged a trailer in the Atlantic Garden Complex in Egg Harbor Township. About 2,000 homes and businesses lost power in the county. In Cumberland County, a large pine tree fell onto a moving vehicle in Vineland. The occupants were not hurt. About 1,000 homes and businesses in the county lost power. In Gloucester County, a downed tree damaged the sunroom of a home in Glassboro. About 3,300 homes and businesses lost power. ||Peak wind gusts included 75 mph in Pennsville (Salem County), 68 mph in Brigantine (Atlantic County), 65 mph at High Point (Sussex County), 63 mph in Wantage (Sussex County) and Cape May (Cape May County), 54 mph at Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), Atlantic City International Airport and Millville (Cumberland County), 51 mph in Point Pleasant (Ocean County) and 46 mph in Trenton (Mercer County).||The high winds were caused by the large pressure difference between a rapidly intensifying Alberta Clipper type low pressure system moving through New England and a strong high pressure system approaching from the upper Mississippi Valley.

As a low pressure system intensified as it moved from the Gulf of Maine into the Canadian Maritimes overnight on the 21st, strong gusty west winds developed across New Jersey. The strongest winds occurred from the late evening on the 21st into the pre-dawn hours on the 22nd and were concentrated in the eastern and higher terrain locations in the state. The strong winds caused some isolated power outages. The most concentrated outages (about 500) were reported in Morris County in Flanders, Mountain Lakes and Parsippany.  ||Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and included 57 mph at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), 54 mph at the Cape May Ferry Terminal (Cape May County), 52 mph in Wantage (Sussex County), 50 mph in Keansburg (Monmouth County), 48 mph at High Point (Sussex County) and Barnegat (Ocean County) and 41 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County) and Wrightstown (Burlington County).

A wintry mix of precipitation fell during the first half of the day into the early afternoon on the 21st across most of New Jersey. Along the immediate shore, precipitation fell as plain rain. Precipitation in the central and southern part of the state inland fell mainly as freezing rain with a little bit of sleet. Ice accretions averaged one tenth to two tenths of an inch. In the Raritan River Basin precipitation started as snow, but changed to sleet and freezing rain quickly. Snow and sleet accumulations averaged around half an inch and ice accretions between one tenth and two tenths of an inch. In Warren and Morris Counties, precipitation started as snow, but changed to sleet and freezing rain before it ended. Snow and sleet accumulations averaged one to three inches. In Sussex County, precipitation fell mainly as snow with accumulations averaging around three inches. ||The wintry mix and freezing rain made for slippery travel on untreated roadways. Because the 21st was a Sunday morning, the number of reported accidents was relatively low. One of the worst reported accidents occurred in Burlington County on the New Jersey Turnpike near Bordentown where two buses and two vehicles collided. Seventeen people suffered minor injuries.||Actual snow and sleet accumulations included 2.9 inches in Butler (Morris County), 2.4 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 2.2 inches in Rockaway (Morris County), 1.3 inches in Blairstown (Warren County) and 1.1 inches in Branchburg (Somerset County).||The wintry mix was caused by a low pressure system that moved from the Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 20th east into southwest Ontario Province on the morning of the 21st. A secondary low pressure system formed on the system's triple point over North Carolina that morning and moved northeast passing Cape Cod as it became the primary low pressure system early in the evening on the 21st. The secondary low pressure system helped maintain surface winds from the northeast and kept temperatures below freezing until the event ended on the morning of the 21st.

A wintry mix of sleet and freezing rain fell during the first half of the morning on the 24th across central and northern New Jersey. The resulting glaze on untreated roadways led to numerous accidents. Precipitation was spottier across south central New Jersey, but was steadier north of Interstate 195 which just about divides the state in half. Ice accretions in the southern part of the state were less than one-tenth of an inch while ice accretions across the northern part of the state averaged between one-tenth and two-tenths of an inch. In the southern part of the state, precipitation briefly started as sleet a little after Midnight EST before changing to freezing rain. In the northern part of the state, precipitation started as sleet between 1 a.m. and 4 a.m. EST and changed over to freezing rain in all areas by 4 a.m. EST. Temperatures continued to rise that morning and by 7 a.m. EST, the freezing rain changed to plain rain in the southern part of the state. Between 7 a.m. and 9 a.m. EST precipitation changed to plain rain in the northern part of the state.||The recent unseasonably cold weather made it easier for slippery conditions to develop on untreated roadways and led to a rash of accidents. In Morris County, Interstate 287 was closed during the morning because of accidents. Accidents occurred on Interstate 80 in Mount Olive Township. Numerous crashes also were reported on Interstate 80 in Warren County and on Interstate 78 in Warren Township and Interstate 287 in Bernards Township (both in Somerset County). In Middlesex County, the Garden State Parkway was closed before sunrise at the Driscoll Bridge because of nearly twenty accidents on the span. In all about 40 accidents were reported on the Garden State Parkway in Middlesex and Monmouth Counties between 230 a.m. EST and 6 a.m. EST. In Monmouth County, in Manalapan Township, six accidents were reported. In Ocean County, in Lakewood, multiple accidents were reported. One motorist was injured after striking a pole. In a separate incident, a woman carrying an infant slipped and fell injuring herself. The number of accidents slowed after sunrise.||The freezing rain and sleet was caused by a low pressure system and its approaching warm front. Precipitation developed preceding the warm front. The arctic high pressure system over the region left enough cold air near the surface for precipitation to start as sleet and freezing rain.

Strong to high winds buffeted New Jersey from the late morning through the evening on the 31st. Hardest hit by the wind were the extreme southern part of the state as well as Sussex County. The highest wind gusts occurred during the afternoon of the 31st, except in Sussex where the highest wind gusts occurred during the evening. Numerous tree limbs, trees and power lines were knocked down. The majority of the 13,000 customers losing power were in the southern part of the state.||The most serious reported injury occurred in Elsinboro Township in Salem County. A 59-year-old woman broke her neck after a downed tree fell onto her vehicle. Elsewhere in the county, in Pennsville a downed tree damaged a home. About 1,100 homes and businesses lost power mainly in Elsinboro, Mannington and Carneys Point. In Atlantic County, a downed tree badly damaged a trailer in the Atlantic Garden Complex in Egg Harbor Township. About 2,000 homes and businesses lost power in the county. In Cumberland County, a large pine tree fell onto a moving vehicle in Vineland. The occupants were not hurt. About 1,000 homes and businesses in the county lost power. In Gloucester County, a downed tree damaged the sunroom of a home in Glassboro. About 3,300 homes and businesses lost power. ||Peak wind gusts included 75 mph in Pennsville (Salem County), 68 mph in Brigantine (Atlantic County), 65 mph at High Point (Sussex County), 63 mph in Wantage (Sussex County) and Cape May (Cape May County), 54 mph at Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), Atlantic City International Airport and Millville (Cumberland County), 51 mph in Point Pleasant (Ocean County) and 46 mph in Trenton (Mercer County).||The high winds were caused by the large pressure difference between a rapidly intensifying Alberta Clipper type low pressure system moving through New England and a strong high pressure system approaching from the upper Mississippi Valley.

Low pressure tracked northeast from the Gulf of Mexico and over the local area along a stationary boundary from December 11th through December 12th. A prolonged period of rain, which was heavy at times, caused widespread flooding across Northeast New Jersey with total rainfall amounts ranging from 2.75 inches to 4 inches. Both the Saddle River at Lodi and the Ramapo River at Mahwah exceeded flood stage as did many of the smaller streams across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Saddle River at Lodi exceeded its flood stage of 6.0 ft at 5 am.  The river crested at 6.36 ft. at 9 am, but Route 46 was closed upstream of the gage due to flooding. Total rainfall amounts in Bergen County ranged from 2.75 inches to 3.85 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty west winds accompanied and persisted behind a cold front from around 9 a.m. EST into the middle of the afternoon on the 30th across New Jersey. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and most of them occurred during the second half of the morning. In Bellmawr (Camden County), a downed tree landed on the shoulder of exit 3 of the New Jersey Turnpike. Traffic was not affected. Public Service Electric and Gas reported scattered power outages because of the strong winds. A strong cold front moved across the Northeast during the early morning hours of December 25. Gusty northwest winds of 40 to 50 mph behind the front toppled a 40 foot tree onto a car traveling on the Garden State Parkway in Bloomfield, New Jersey, at approximately 0110EST.  A 42-year-old man and 70-year-old woman were killed; and 13- and 8-year-old girls were injured in the car.

Low pressure tracked northeast from the Gulf of Mexico and over the local area along a stationary boundary from December 11th through December 12th. A prolonged period of rain, which was heavy at times, caused widespread flooding across Northeast New Jersey with total rainfall amounts ranging from 2.75 inches to 4 inches. Both the Saddle River at Lodi and the Ramapo River at Mahwah exceeded flood stage as did many of the smaller streams across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Springfield Ave. was closed in Irvington due to flooding.

A prolonged period of rain, which was heavy at times, caused widespread flooding across Northeast New Jersey with total rainfall amounts ranging from 2.75 inches to 4 inches. Both the Saddle River at Lodi and the Ramapo River at Mahwah exceeded flood stage as did many of the smaller streams across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rahway River at Springfield exceeded its flood stage of 5.5 ft. at 12:15 am and crested at 6.36 ft. at 5:45 am. This led to flooding and eventual closing of Route 22 in Springfield.

A clipper type low pressure system brought snow to Sussex County and snow and then rain across the Passaic and Raritan River Basins in northern New Jersey on February 29th into March 1st. Accumulations averaged around three inches across the higher terrain and around one inch elsewhere. Snow began during the evening on February 29th. The snow changed to rain after Midnight EST on March 1st, except for Sussex County and some other higher terrain locations where it remained all snow. Precipitation ended before sunrise on March 1st. The change to rain, lighter overall precipitation amounts and marginal (around freezing) temperatures limited accumulations in lower terrain locations. ||Actual accumulations included 3.8 inches in High Point (Sussex County), 3.3 inches in Marcella (Morris County), 3.0 inches in Butler (Morris County), 2.6 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 1.6 inches in Hackettstown (Warren County), 1.5 inches in Flemington (Hunterdon County), 1.3 inches in Chatham (Morris County) and 1.0 inch in South Plainfield (Middlesex County). ||The clipper low pressure system moved from North Dakota on the morning of February 28th east into southern Wisconsin on the morning of February 29th and through the Saint Lawrence River Valley on March 1st.

Lowest tides included -2.28 feet below mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), -2.27 feet below mean lower low water at Barnegat Light (Ocean County), -2.18 feet below mean lower low water at Atlantic City (Atlantic County), -2.29 feet below mean lower low water in Absecon (Atlantic County) and -1.80 feet below mean lower low water at the Cape May Ferry Terminal (Cape May County). Blowout tides begin at -1.80 feet below mean lower low water. The persistent west to northwest winds were caused by the pressure gradient (difference) between a high pressure system that was moving east from the Northern Plains and Great Lakes and an intense low pressure system over the Canadian Maritimes.

Runoff from heavy rain caused flooding along sections of the Millstone and North Branch of the Raritan River in Somerset County from the late evening on the 1st into the 2nd. Storm totals averaged around 1.50 inches. Rain began during the morning of the first and fell at a steady pace throughout much of the day until it ended that evening. The heaviest rain fell around 6 p.m. EST.  The Millstone River at Griggstown was above its 10 foot flood stage from Midnight EST through 1145 p.m. EST on the 2nd. It crested at 10.69 feet at 1115 a.m. EST. The North Branch of the Raritan River at the village of South Branch was above its 7 foot flood from 1016 p.m. EST on the 2nd through 2 a.m. EST on the 2nd. It crested at 7.34 feet 1115 p.m. EST on the 1st. Storm totals included 1.80 inches in Blackwells Mills, 1.70 inches in Bound Brook and Belle Mead, 1.60 inches in Neshanic, 1.40 inches in Somerville and 1.31 inches in Pottersville. ||The low pressure system that caused the rain moved from Mississippi on the evening of January 31st northeast through the Ohio valley on the morning of the 1st. It reached western Pennsylvania during the afternoon of the 1st and passed near Scranton, Pennsylvania at 7 p.m. EST on the 1st before heading northeast into New York State.

The combination of an upper air disturbance and a weak wave of low pressure that formed on an offshore cold front brought a wintry mix of light precipitation to the extreme southeast part of New Jersey overnight on the 13th. Rain fell throughout the day on the 13th. As colder air moved south on northwest winds, precipitation changed from rain to freezing rain to sleet and then snow. Precipitation ended just before sunrise on the 14th. Storm total water equivalents were less than one tenth of an inch. Snow accumulations were less than an inch and ice accretions were minimal. But even so, the wintry mix caused problems on untreated and less traveled roadways in the area.

Lowest tides included -2.28 feet below mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), -2.27 feet below mean lower low water at Barnegat Light (Ocean County), -2.18 feet below mean lower low water at Atlantic City (Atlantic County), -2.29 feet below mean lower low water in Absecon (Atlantic County) and -1.80 feet below mean lower low water at the Cape May Ferry Terminal (Cape May County). Blowout tides begin at -1.80 feet below mean lower low water. The persistent west to northwest winds were caused by the pressure gradient (difference) between a high pressure system that was moving east from the Northern Plains and Great Lakes and an intense low pressure system over the Canadian Maritimes.

A strong cold front that moved through northwestern New Jersey late in the day on the 9th brought scattered snow showers to the area. Total snow accumulations of one to two inches fell across parts of northwestern New Jersey, mainly in Sussex County. Specific accumulations included 1.9 inches in Wantage (Sussex County) and 0.3 inches in Butler (Morris County).

A clipper type low pressure system brought snow to Sussex County and snow and then rain across the Passaic and Raritan River Basins in northern New Jersey on February 29th into March 1st. Accumulations averaged around three inches across the higher terrain and around one inch elsewhere. Snow began during the evening on February 29th. The snow changed to rain after Midnight EST on March 1st, except for Sussex County and some other higher terrain locations where it remained all snow. Precipitation ended before sunrise on March 1st. The change to rain, lighter overall precipitation amounts and marginal (around freezing) temperatures limited accumulations in lower terrain locations. ||Actual accumulations included 3.8 inches in High Point (Sussex County), 3.3 inches in Marcella (Morris County), 3.0 inches in Butler (Morris County), 2.6 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 1.6 inches in Hackettstown (Warren County), 1.5 inches in Flemington (Hunterdon County), 1.3 inches in Chatham (Morris County) and 1.0 inch in South Plainfield (Middlesex County). ||The clipper low pressure system moved from North Dakota on the morning of February 28th east into southern Wisconsin on the morning of February 29th and through the Saint Lawrence River Valley on March 1st.

The combination of an upper air disturbance and a weak wave of low pressure that formed on an offshore cold front brought a wintry mix of light precipitation to the extreme southeast part of New Jersey overnight on the 13th. Rain fell throughout the day on the 13th. As colder air moved south on northwest winds, precipitation changed from rain to freezing rain to sleet and then snow. Precipitation ended just before sunrise on the 14th. Storm total water equivalents were less than one tenth of an inch. Snow accumulations were less than an inch and ice accretions were minimal. But even so, the wintry mix caused problems on untreated and less traveled roadways in the area.

A wintry mix of precipitation fell during the first half of the day into the early afternoon on the 21st across most of New Jersey. Along the immediate shore, precipitation fell as plain rain. Precipitation in the central and southern part of the state inland fell mainly as freezing rain with a little bit of sleet. Ice accretions averaged one tenth to two tenths of an inch. In the Raritan River Basin precipitation started as snow, but changed to sleet and freezing rain quickly. Snow and sleet accumulations averaged around half an inch and ice accretions between one tenth and two tenths of an inch. In Warren and Morris Counties, precipitation started as snow, but changed to sleet and freezing rain before it ended. Snow and sleet accumulations averaged one to three inches. In Sussex County, precipitation fell mainly as snow with accumulations averaging around three inches. ||The wintry mix and freezing rain made for slippery travel on untreated roadways. Because the 21st was a Sunday morning, the number of reported accidents was relatively low. One of the worst reported accidents occurred in Burlington County on the New Jersey Turnpike near Bordentown where two buses and two vehicles collided. Seventeen people suffered minor injuries.||Actual snow and sleet accumulations included 2.9 inches in Butler (Morris County), 2.4 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 2.2 inches in Rockaway (Morris County), 1.3 inches in Blairstown (Warren County) and 1.1 inches in Branchburg (Somerset County).||The wintry mix was caused by a low pressure system that moved from the Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 20th east into southwest Ontario Province on the morning of the 21st. A secondary low pressure system formed on the system's triple point over North Carolina that morning and moved northeast passing Cape Cod as it became the primary low pressure system early in the evening on the 21st. The secondary low pressure system helped maintain surface winds from the northeast and kept temperatures below freezing until the event ended on the morning of the 21st.

As a low pressure system intensified as it moved from the Gulf of Maine into the Canadian Maritimes overnight on the 21st, strong gusty west winds developed across New Jersey. The strongest winds occurred from the late evening on the 21st into the pre-dawn hours on the 22nd and were concentrated in the eastern and higher terrain locations in the state. The strong winds caused some isolated power outages. The most concentrated outages (about 500) were reported in Morris County in Flanders, Mountain Lakes and Parsippany.  ||Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and included 57 mph at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), 54 mph at the Cape May Ferry Terminal (Cape May County), 52 mph in Wantage (Sussex County), 50 mph in Keansburg (Monmouth County), 48 mph at High Point (Sussex County) and Barnegat (Ocean County) and 41 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County) and Wrightstown (Burlington County).

A wintry mix of sleet and freezing rain fell during the first half of the morning on the 24th across central and northern New Jersey. The resulting glaze on untreated roadways led to numerous accidents. Precipitation was spottier across south central New Jersey, but was steadier north of Interstate 195 which just about divides the state in half. Ice accretions in the southern part of the state were less than one-tenth of an inch while ice accretions across the northern part of the state averaged between one-tenth and two-tenths of an inch. In the southern part of the state, precipitation briefly started as sleet a little after Midnight EST before changing to freezing rain. In the northern part of the state, precipitation started as sleet between 1 a.m. and 4 a.m. EST and changed over to freezing rain in all areas by 4 a.m. EST. Temperatures continued to rise that morning and by 7 a.m. EST, the freezing rain changed to plain rain in the southern part of the state. Between 7 a.m. and 9 a.m. EST precipitation changed to plain rain in the northern part of the state.||The recent unseasonably cold weather made it easier for slippery conditions to develop on untreated roadways and led to a rash of accidents. In Morris County, Interstate 287 was closed during the morning because of accidents. Accidents occurred on Interstate 80 in Mount Olive Township. Numerous crashes also were reported on Interstate 80 in Warren County and on Interstate 78 in Warren Township and Interstate 287 in Bernards Township (both in Somerset County). In Middlesex County, the Garden State Parkway was closed before sunrise at the Driscoll Bridge because of nearly twenty accidents on the span. In all about 40 accidents were reported on the Garden State Parkway in Middlesex and Monmouth Counties between 230 a.m. EST and 6 a.m. EST. In Monmouth County, in Manalapan Township, six accidents were reported. In Ocean County, in Lakewood, multiple accidents were reported. One motorist was injured after striking a pole. In a separate incident, a woman carrying an infant slipped and fell injuring herself. The number of accidents slowed after sunrise.||The freezing rain and sleet was caused by a low pressure system and its approaching warm front. Precipitation developed preceding the warm front. The arctic high pressure system over the region left enough cold air near the surface for precipitation to start as sleet and freezing rain.

In Middlesex County, the Garden State Parkway was closed before sunrise at the Driscoll Bridge because of nearly twenty accidents on the span. In all about 40 accidents were reported on the Garden State Parkway in Middlesex and Monmouth Counties between 230 a.m. EST and 6 a.m. EST. In Monmouth County, in Manalapan Township, six accidents were reported. In Ocean County, in Lakewood, multiple accidents were reported. One motorist was injured after striking a pole. In a separate incident, a woman carrying an infant slipped and fell injuring herself. The number of accidents slowed after sunrise.||The freezing rain and sleet was caused by a low pressure system and its approaching warm front. Precipitation developed preceding the warm front. A wintry mix of sleet and freezing rain fell during the first half of the morning on the 24th across central and northern New Jersey. The resulting glaze on untreated roadways led to numerous accidents. Precipitation was spottier across south central New Jersey, but was steadier north of Interstate 195 which just about divides the state in half. Ice accretions in the southern part of the state were less than one-tenth of an inch while ice accretions across the northern part of the state averaged between one-tenth and two-tenths of an inch. In the southern part of the state, precipitation briefly started as sleet a little after Midnight EST before changing to freezing rain. In the northern part of the state, precipitation started as sleet between 1 a.m. and 4 a.m. EST and changed over to freezing rain in all areas by 4 a.m. EST. Temperatures continued to rise that morning and by 7 a.m. EST, the freezing rain changed to plain rain in the southern part of the state. Between 7 a.m. and 9 a.m. EST precipitation changed to plain rain in the northern part of the state.||The recent unseasonably cold weather made it easier for slippery conditions to develop on untreated roadways and led to a rash of accidents. An Alberta Clipper type low pressure system that moved across northern New Jersey during the daytime on the 31st dropped about 1 to 2 inches of snow across northern sections of the state. The snow fell in bursts during the morning and first half of the afternoon. Strong gusty winds accompanied the snow showers in the afternoon and caused reduced visibilities. Quick accumulations occurred on roadways as temperatures fell during the snow. No serious accidents were reported. Accumulations included 2.1 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 2.0 inches in Newton (Sussex County), 1.8 inches in Colts Neck (Monmouth County), 1.4 inches in Marcella (Morris County), 1.3 inches in Rockaway (Morris County) and 1.0 inch in Blairstown and Hackettstown (both Warren County) and Boonton (Morris County).

The persistent and strong west to northwest offshore flow caused blowout tides to occur in upper Delaware Bay as well as along tidal sections of the Delaware River during the evening low tide on the 31st. The low tide in Philadelphia (Pennsylvania) reached 2.0 feet below mean lower low water. The low tide at Reedy Point (Delaware) was 2.4 feet below mean lower low water. Blowout tides start at 1.8 feet below mean lower low water.

Strong southerly winds of up to 30 mph downed a silver maple tree on a man in Orange, Essex County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fifty foot silver maple tree cracked at its base and fell on a man walking along the sidewalk on South Center Street, in Orange.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty west to northwest winds occurred during the late morning and afternoon on the 7th across New Jersey. The strong winds helped fuel a fire that badly damaged a home in Monroe Township in Gloucester County. Peak wind gusts averaged 45 to 50 mph and included 55 mph in High Point (Sussex County), 51 mph at the Cape May Ferry Terminal (Cape May County), 50 mph at the Atlantic City Marina (Atlantic County), 47 mph in Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), 46 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport, 45 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), 44 mph in Trenton (Mercer County) and 43 mph in Millvlle (Cumberland County). The strong winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system that was moving through the Saint Lawrence Valley and a high pressure system building east from the central Mississippi Valley.

As a low pressure system intensified as it moved from the Gulf of Maine into the Canadian Maritimes overnight on the 21st, strong gusty west winds developed across New Jersey. The strongest winds occurred from the late evening on the 21st into the pre-dawn hours on the 22nd and were concentrated in the eastern and higher terrain locations in the state. The strong winds caused some isolated power outages. The most concentrated outages (about 500) were reported in Morris County in Flanders, Mountain Lakes and Parsippany.  ||Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and included 57 mph at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), 54 mph at the Cape May Ferry Terminal (Cape May County), 52 mph in Wantage (Sussex County), 50 mph in Keansburg (Monmouth County), 48 mph at High Point (Sussex County) and Barnegat (Ocean County) and 41 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County) and Wrightstown (Burlington County).

A wintry mix of precipitation fell during the first half of the day into the early afternoon on the 21st across most of New Jersey. Along the immediate shore, precipitation fell as plain rain. Precipitation in the central and southern part of the state inland fell mainly as freezing rain with a little bit of sleet. Ice accretions averaged one tenth to two tenths of an inch. In the Raritan River Basin precipitation started as snow, but changed to sleet and freezing rain quickly. Snow and sleet accumulations averaged around half an inch and ice accretions between one tenth and two tenths of an inch. In Warren and Morris Counties, precipitation started as snow, but changed to sleet and freezing rain before it ended. Snow and sleet accumulations averaged one to three inches. In Sussex County, precipitation fell mainly as snow with accumulations averaging around three inches. ||The wintry mix and freezing rain made for slippery travel on untreated roadways. Because the 21st was a Sunday morning, the number of reported accidents was relatively low. One of the worst reported accidents occurred in Burlington County on the New Jersey Turnpike near Bordentown where two buses and two vehicles collided. Seventeen people suffered minor injuries.||Actual snow and sleet accumulations included 2.9 inches in Butler (Morris County), 2.4 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 2.2 inches in Rockaway (Morris County), 1.3 inches in Blairstown (Warren County) and 1.1 inches in Branchburg (Somerset County).||The wintry mix was caused by a low pressure system that moved from the Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 20th east into southwest Ontario Province on the morning of the 21st. A secondary low pressure system formed on the system's triple point over North Carolina that morning and moved northeast passing Cape Cod as it became the primary low pressure system early in the evening on the 21st. The secondary low pressure system helped maintain surface winds from the northeast and kept temperatures below freezing until the event ended on the morning of the 21st.

Strong, gusty northwest winds occurred across central and northern New Jersey during the morning of the 30th. The associated low pressure system and the subsequent tight pressure gradient (difference) between it and the approaching high pressure system was not that large. Thus winds diminished during the afternoon of the 30th.

An Alberta Clipper spread light snow across extreme southern New Jersey during the afternoon and evening of the 20th. The snow started during the early afternoon and ended by the middle of the evening. Accumulations averaged 1 to 2 inches and included 2.3 inches in Cape May (Cape May County), 2.0 inches in Rio Grande (Cape May County), 1.5 inches in Millville (Cumberland County), 1.4 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County) and 1.3 inches in Woodbine (Cape May County). The snow was caused by a low pressure system known as an Alberta Clipper that went from the Kansas/Nebraska border on the evening of the 19th east through the central Ohio Valley during the night of the 19th and morning of the 20th. It crossed the mountains of West Virginia early in the afternoon of the 20th and was located near Norfolk, Virginia at 7 p.m. EST on the 20th. It raced offshore from there and was nearly 300 miles east of Virginia, Beach Virginia at 1 a.m. EST on the 21st. The fast movement once it approached and moved offshore prevented heavier snow from falling.

An arctic front swept across New Jersey during the morning hours of the 10th. Northwesterly winds increased in its wake and became rather gusty during the afternoon and evening hours. The strongest wind gusts reached between 60 and 70 mph along the shore and averaged around 50 mph inland. A wintry mix of precipitation including some heavy snow fell across New Jersey on the 22nd. The heaviest snow occurred near the Pennsylvania border. Precipitation started as snow throughout the area around or just after Midnight EST on the 22nd. As warmer air moved in aloft, precipitation changed over to sleet during the morning in the southern half of the state and during the late morning and early afternoon in the northern half of the state. Precipitation then changed to plain rain during the second half of the morning in the southeast part of the state, but freezing rain in the southwest part of the state. The change to freezing rain occurred during the afternoon in the northern half of the state. Precipitation then changed to plain rain during the afternoon in the southwest, central and Raritan River Basin areas of the state. In the northwest part of the state, intermittent light freezing rain continued until the event ended during the late evening of the 22nd. Snow and sleet accumulations averaged 1 to 3 inches in the southeast part of the state and 3 to 8 inches elsewhere. Ice accretions were generally around one-tenth of an inch.||The wintry precipitation caused many traveling problems around the region. Many schools in the state (except for the southeast portion) never opened. The nearby Philadelphia International Airport experienced delays of up to four hours. The New Jersey Turnpike imposed a 35 mph speed limit. This was raised to 45 mph at Noon EST on the 22nd as the snow slowed or stopped and conditions began to improve. New Jersey Transit trains and buses experienced delays of about 20 minutes on average.||In Morris County, a car and a small box truck were involved in a minor accident in which the two vehicles ended against a guardrail on Interstate 287 North in Parsippany. Dozens of cars collided, several overturned and some drivers were injured, which forced the closure of Interstate 287 South near exit 57 during the night of the 22nd. This was due to melting snow which re-froze into a sheet of ice on the road. A vehicle overturned at about 8:20 PM EST on the 22nd on Interstate 80 East between the Howard Boulevard exit in Mount Arlington and the Mount Hope-Dover exit. The accident closed one lane and icing was suspected to be the cause. Jersey Central Power and Light reported some outages in the Budd Lake section of Mount Olive (Morris County) and in Blairstown (Warren County). ||In Burlington County, snow and slush covered roads led to plenty of fender benders and spinouts across the county, however no serious injuries were reported. In Camden County, some high school sporting events were postponed or cancelled. In Middlesex County, one accident in Sayreville around 8:00 AM EST resulted in an overturned vehicle. The driver was freed from the vehicle shortly after the accident.||Actual accumulations included 8.7 inches in Vernon (Sussex County), 7.5 inches in Milton (Morris County) and Blairstown (Warren County), 7.0 inches in Tewksbury (Hunterdon County) and Marcella (Morris County), 6.5 inches in Carteret (Middlesex County), Sparta (Sussex County) and Bridgewater (Somerset County), 6.1 inches in Somerville (Somerset County), 6.0 inches in New Brunswick (Middlesex County) and Hackettstown (Warren County), 5.5 inches in Frenchtown (Hunterdon County), Whippany (Morris County) and Trenton (Mercer County), 5.0 inches in Florence (Burlington County) and Sea Bright (Monmouth County), 4.4 inches in Colts Neck (Monmouth County), 4.0 inches in Princeton (Mercer County), 3.8 inches in Atco and Somerdale (Camden County), 3.6 inches in Southampton (Burlington County), 3.5 inches in Williamstown (Gloucester County) and Bayville and Manchester (Ocean County), 2.7 inches in Estell Manor (Atlantic County), 2.4 inches in Pittsgrove (Salem County), 2.3 inches in Bridgeton (Cumberland County), 2.0 inches in Bridgeton (Cumberland County), 1.8 inches in Seaville (Cape May County), 1.6 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport, 1.4 inches in Leesburg (Cumberland County) and 1.0 inch in North Cape May (Cape May County). ||The wintry mix was caused by a low pressure system that formed along the Gulf Coast States during the afternoon of the 21st. This low pressure system moved northeast and reached Mississippi on the morning of the 22nd and the Tennessee Valley during the early afternoon of the 22nd. A secondary low pressure formed along the South Carolina coast that morning and reached Cape Hatteras early that afternoon. The secondary low pressure system continued to move northeast and by 7 p.m. EST on the 22nd was located about 125 miles east of Long Beach Island, New Jersey. It passed just south of Nova Scotia overnight. The surface high pressure system that preceded the low left a cold and dry air mass in place for precipitation to start as snow. Since the high pressure system moved offshore, enough warm air in came in from the Atlantic to change the precipitation over to plain rain in the central and southern part of the state. But, farther north, northeast winds fed colder air into the state and prevented temperatures from rising above freezing.

An Alberta Clipper spread light snow across extreme southern New Jersey during the afternoon and evening of the 20th. The snow started during the early afternoon and ended by the middle of the evening. Accumulations averaged 1 to 2 inches and included 2.3 inches in Cape May (Cape May County), 2.0 inches in Rio Grande (Cape May County), 1.5 inches in Millville (Cumberland County), 1.4 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County) and 1.3 inches in Woodbine (Cape May County). The snow was caused by a low pressure system known as an Alberta Clipper that went from the Kansas/Nebraska border on the evening of the 19th east through the central Ohio Valley during the night of the 19th and morning of the 20th. It crossed the mountains of West Virginia early in the afternoon of the 20th and was located near Norfolk, Virginia at 7 p.m. EST on the 20th. It raced offshore from there and was nearly 300 miles east of Virginia, Beach Virginia at 1 a.m. EST on the 21st. The fast movement once it approached and moved offshore prevented heavier snow from falling.

A steady rain caused flooding along sections of the Rockaway and Passaic Rivers in Morris County from the early evening on the 18th into the afternoon of the 20th. The rivers were already running high from the flooding of the previous week. Rain began falling on the evening of the 17th and ended during the early afternoon on the 18th. Storm totals averaged around one inch and included 1.28 inches in Berkshire Valley, 1.22 inches in West Wharton and 1.10 inches in Boonton. ||The Rockaway River below the Boonton Reservoir was above its 5 foot flood stage from 720 p.m. EST on the 18th through 732 p.m. EST on the 19th. It crested at 5.37 feet at 9 a.m. EST on the 19th. The Passaic River at Pine Brook was above its 19 foot flood stage from 725 a.m. EST on the 19th through 345 p.m. EST on the 20th. It crested at 19.10 feet at 730 p.m. EST on the 19th. The Passaic River at Two Bridges was above its 9 foot flood stage from 130 p.m. EST on the 19th through 1030 a.m. EST on the 20th. It crested at 9.09 feet at 1145 p.m. EST on the 19th.||The steady rain was caused by a low pressure system that moved from the lower Missouri Valley on the morning of the 17th northeast through the Saint Lawrence Valley on the morning of the 18th.

A wintry mix of precipitation including some heavy snow fell across New Jersey on the 22nd. The heaviest snow occurred near the Pennsylvania border. Precipitation started as snow throughout the area around or just after Midnight EST on the 22nd. Precipitation then changed to plain rain during the second half of the morning in the southeast part of the state, but freezing rain in the southwest part of the state. The change to freezing rain occurred during the afternoon in the northern half of the state. Precipitation then changed to plain rain during the afternoon in the southwest, central and Raritan River Basin areas of the state. In the northwest part of the state, intermittent light freezing rain continued until the event ended during the late evening of the 22nd.
