Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph. The strongest wind gusts occurred toward and along the shore.||Peak wind gusts included 50 mph in Cape May Harbor (Cape May County), Barnegat Light(Ocean County) and Keansburg (Monmouth County), 48 mph in Wrightstown (Burlington County), Seaside Heights (Ocean County) and Belmar (Monmouth County), 47 mph in Atlantic City (Atlantic County), 43 mph in Chatham (Morris County) and 41 mph in Trenton (Mercer County) and Millville (Cumberland).   ||A low pressure system moved northeast from the Tennessee Valley on the morning of the 19th into Eastern Pennsylvania during the evening on the 19th and passed near New York City just past midnight EST on the 20th. The low pressure system intensified as it moved through southern New England on the morning of the 20th and reached Nova Scotia on the evening of the 20th. As a high pressure system from the lower Tennessee Valley built northeast, wind speeds began to diminish late in the afternoon on the 20th.

A complex low pressure system over the Gulf Coast Friday morning March 7th, interacted with a frontal boundary approaching from the Ohio Valley as it moved northeast. The frontal boundary became nearly stationary across the coastal plain Friday night and a wave of low pressure developed along it. This low passed over the region during the overnight period of March 7th to March 8th followed by the main area of low pressure on Saturday, March 8th, with rainfall totals from both systems between 1 1/2 and 2 1/2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ho-Ho-Kus Brook and Hackensack River both exceeded their flood stages producing minor flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The runoff from the heavy rain caused flash flooding of smaller streams as well as basement and poor drainage flooding across much of central and northwest New Jersey during the afternoon of the 8th. The region was primed for flooding by another heavy rain event that occurred on the 5th. The runoff from the heavy rain caused flooding to continue through the 10th in the Raritan Basin and through the 13th in the Passaic Basin. Rain began falling during the afternoon on the 7th and did not end until the early evening on the 8th. It fell at its heaviest between 10 a.m. EST and 3 p.m. EST on the 8th. Storm totals ranged from one to four inches with the highest amounts in Morris County.||Please note, some of the flooding persisted through the crossover between eastern standard and eastern daylight time on the night of March 8th.||The heavy rain was caused by a low pressure system that developed in the northeastern Gulf of Mexico on the 7th. It deepened to a 997 millibar low by the time it moved slowly northeast into West Virginia at 7 a.m. EST on the 8th. At 1 p.m. EST on the 8th, there were a pair of 992 millibar lows, one near Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania and the other in northern Virginia. At 4 p.m. EST on the 8th, they combined into one over southeast Pennsylvania and deepened to 987 millibars. At 7 p.m. EST the low had deepened to 985 millibars and was near New York City. Its associated cold front had cleared Delaware and New Jersey and ended the rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the heavy rain earlier in the day led to flooding along the South Branch of the Raritan River. The South Branch of the Raritan River at High Bridge was above its 10 foot flood stage from 1215 a.m. EST through 3 a.m. EDT on the 9th. It crested at 10.04 feet at 115 a.m. EST. Farther downstream, the South Branch of the Raritan River at Stanton was above its 8 foot flood stage from 508 p.m. EST on the 8th through 825 a.m. EDT on the 9th. It crested at 8.69 feet at 745 p.m. EST on the 8th. Event precipitation totals included 2.91 inches in Flemington, 2.38 inches in Stockton and 2.36 inches in Califon.

The combination of a prolonged period of rain and melting snow caused poor drainage flooding throughout northwestern New Jersey as well as flooding of some of the rivers and streams. Storm totals were 2 to 3 inches and generally around 1 inch elsewhere in New Jersey. Rain began during the morning of the 4th and fell at its heaviest from Midnight EST through 6 a.m. EST on the 5th. The rain ended on the morning of the 5th.||Precipitation was caused by a low pressure system that moved from the Tennessee Valley on the morning of the 4th and crossed West Virginia early in the evening on the 4th. The low pressure system moved northeast and was located near Syracuse, New York at 7 a.m. EST on the 5th and along the New Hampshire and Vermont border early in the afternoon on the 5th. Precipitation ended as its cold front moved through the area during the morning of the 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavier rain and snow melt in Morris and Hunterdon Counties caused flooding along part of the North Branch of the Raritan River. The North Branch of the Raritan River at the village of South Branch was above its 7 foot flood stage from 923 a.m. EST through 250 p.m. EST on the 5th. It crested at 7.5 feet at 1145 a.m. EST. Precipitation event storm totals included 1.59 inches in Clinton (Hunterdon County), 1.02 inches in Far Hills (Somerset County) and 0.93 inches in Somerville (Somerset County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low tracked across the Tri-State on the 8th, producing damaging winds across Northeast New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on a house in Fair Lawn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low tracked across the Tri-State on the 8th, producing damaging winds across Northeast New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On Lakeside Drive in Ramsey, a tree fell on a house, and multiple power lines were down, with one utility pole snapped in half.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds from a possible dry microburst overturned a portable backstop, injuring 3 in Bergen County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A movable backstop overturned froma 30-40 mph windgust, most likely from a dry microburst. 3 minor injuries resulted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached and then moved through during the afternoon and evening hours of the 27th. Scattered thunderstorms developed as a result of a warm and moist air mass ahead of the cold front. One storm quickly intensified as it moved across southern Sussex County during the late afternoon hours, producing penny sized hail in Newton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm quickly intensified as it moved across the southern portion of Sussex County during the late afternoon hours of the 27th. This storm briefly produced hail to the size of pennies in Newton, then moved eastward and developed more into a line of thunderstorms.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from the heavy rain and snow melt caused flooding along the Flat Brook. The Flat Brook at Flatbrookville was above its estimated 6 foot flood stage from 1208 p.m. EST on the 5th through 1222 a.m. EST on the 6th. It crested at 6.75 feet at 515 p.m. EST on the 5th. Storm total precipitation included 2.31 inches in Andover, 2.14 inches in Walkill and 1.85 inches in Sussex.

Event precipitation totals included 2.00 inches in Berkshire Valley, 1.84 inches in West Wharton and 1.50 inches in Boonton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds behind a cold front and saturated grounds from the heavy rain pushed over some weak trees and caused wind damage in northwest New Jersey on the morning of the 5th.  ||In Warren County, in Independence Township, the wind ripped off an aluminum roof to a barn and a piece punctured a 1,000 gallon liquid propane tank. The peak wind gust at High Point in Sussex County was 43 mph.

Strong northwest winds behind a cold front and saturated grounds from the heavy rain pushed over some weak trees and caused wind damage in northwest New Jersey on the morning of the 5th.  ||In Warren County, in Independence Township, the wind ripped off an aluminum roof to a barn and a piece punctured a 1,000 gallon liquid propane tank. The peak wind gust at High Point in Sussex County was 43 mph.

A potent low pressure system in the Ohio Valley during the early morning hours of May 11th moved eastward and gradually weakened. A secondary low formed over northeastern North Carolina during the late afternoon on the 11th and eventually absorbed the other low pressure system, becoming the dominant storm. This storm gradually moved east northeastward late on the 11th into the 12th. Early on the morning of the 12th, the storm was centered over the southern Delmarva. As this storm strengthened fairly rapidly, a strong northeast wind developed and persisted through the evening of the 12th. The prolonged northeasterly flow combined with higher than normal tides, caused moderate tidal flooding and areas of beach erosion in New Jersey. The heaviest rain remained across Delaware and Maryland. By the evening of the 12th, the coastal storm tracked eastward and gradually moved away from the region.||The Nor���easter of May 12th downed trees and wires, and flooded streets in dozens of beach towns. Tidal flooding forced the closure of schools and roads in parts of coastal New Jersey on the 12th and 13th. In Stafford (Ocean County), a truck that was buffeted by the winds got stuck on the Route 72 causeway bridge and blocked traffic. In Ocean County, widespread problems were reported from Stafford Township to Bass River Township due to downed power lines and trees on the 12th, most of which were considered nuisances and annoyances. Minor flooding, which in some cases resulted in road closures were reported. High winds caused the Route 72 Bridge to be closed for an extended period of time late in the morning on the 12th. The bridge was reopened shortly after 4:00 PM EDT on the 12th. Across the island, high winds brought down utility lines and sent debris flying. A billboard for a local ReMax real estate office on East Bay Avenue in Stafford Township was shredded on the 12th due to winds topping nearly 70 mph. In Barnegat Township (Ocean County), nearly 20 trees in various spots of town were knocked down, however there were no power outages or injuries reported. Bayshore Drive was shut down as bay water inundated it in the portion by the public beach and the municipal dock. A tree, which fell on Lower Shore Road, closed that road near the recreation complex. Bay Beach and the municipal dock parking lot were under a foot-and-a-half of water. Some of the older homes reportedly lost some roof shingles as one resident reported them laying in their yard. The strong winds even blew the shopping carts from the Genuardi's supermarket onto West Bay Avenue. ||In Surf City (Ocean County), the Causeway Bridge was closed by authorities from midday to about 5:00 PM EDT on the 12th when the winds approached 65 mph. A truck actually got stuck on the bridge as the driver could not control the drift of the vehicle. The flood-prone and low-lying Tuckerton Beach (Ocean County) experienced increased tides due to the coastal storm, which flooded some areas, and at least two cars were lost. During the morning of the 12th, residents awoke to three feet of water around their homes. There were portable toilets on their sides and floating in the middle of the street, and numerous 20-pound propane tanks also floated around. The strong winds also brought down a couple of power lines and blew a boat off of a boatlift. Some homes lost their siding while others had lost roof shingles. In Beach Haven (Ocean County), the high tide and strong onshore winds allowed water to breach over the street entrance off of Merivale and Nelsen Avenues. On the Bayfront, the water had cleared over some of the bulkheads at Taylor Avenue. The usual flooding occurred along some of the side streets off of Bay Avenue. In Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), extra sand was brought in to fill in beach entrances from Passaic Avenue southward. Severe winds damaged houses throughout the borough, with three separate reports of windows being blown open and one house had siding torn off. The bridges to Long Beach Island (Ocean County) were closed shortly before 11:00 AM EDT on the 12th when a box truck was immobilized in high wind near the crest of the span. At approximately 2:30 PM EDT on the 12th, the wind speed was 67.9 mph on the bridge, according to officials. Also, a section of Long Beach Boulevard was closed due to flooding that reached heights of one and a half feet in some spots. Route 72 was blocked off at Marsha Drive near Beach Haven West on the 12th when the causeway bridge connecting Stafford to Long Beach Island (Ocean County) was shut down because of strong winds. ||The Nor'easter of May 11-12 uprooted a few large trees in the Manasquan area (Monmouth County). Also, tree limbs and branches, as well as trash and recycling, were scattered and strewn around the streets. Sand from the local beaches was also swept up over the boardwalks and deposited onto Ocean Avenue, and other beach roads, as a result of the strong winds. Many area power lines were downed, which caused a number of power outages. Waves crashed over the inlet promenade at Riverside Drive on the afternoon of the 12th, which spilled onto the roadway. The combination of the waves and high winds led trash receptacles along a walkway to take flight. The length of Brielle Road was blocked off as more than a foot of water covered it. Main Street was closed between Potter Avenue and Third Avenue due to flooding. A downed power line over Stockton Lake Boulevard, along with flood conditions from the overflowing lake, caused much of the length of that street to be closed. Manasquan's deputy Office of Emergency Management coordinator reported that a storm surge of 3.5 feet came ashore in the borough.||In Belmar (Monmouth County), many large trees, tree branches and tree limbs had fallen due to the high winds. A handful of utility poles throughout the borough were also damaged from the storm. On the afternoon of the 12th, the ocean's waters reached up to the borough's boardwalk at high tide. A large tree limb snapped off and fell onto a home in the Shark River Hills section of Neptune (Monmouth County) on the 12th. In Bradley Beach (Monmouth County), beachfront was hit with severe beach erosion along Ocean Avenue, with a lot of sand that blew up onto the roadways and boardwalk. The storm caused minor damage to the boardwalk gazebos, with shingles being blown away. Large amounts of sand, some to the depth of two feet, in Sea Girt (Monmouth County) was blown onto the boardwalk and Ocean Avenue. A weather station at Sea Girt's beachfront pavilion measured sustained winds of 60 to 64 mph between 9:30 AM EDT and 10:00 AM EDT on the 12th.||Wall Township (Monmouth County) received several reports of downed trees, and also some debris in the roads along with traffic lights that were not working. Brielle Borough experienced numerous downed wires and utility poles, several downed trees and moderate tidal flooding. The borough of Spring Lake (Monmouth County) and its boardwalk as well as much of Ocean Avenue, was inundated with sand blown in from the beach. Some downed trees were also reported with one downed tree near the intersection of Third and St. Claire avenues, which caused the road to be temporarily closed. Avon-by-the-sea beaches had sand pushed back towards the boardwalk, and crews used front-end loaders to try and spread the sand across the beachfront after the storm passed. Sea Bright (Monmouth County) experienced flooding on the 12th as strong winds and high tides occurred. Approximately 3,000 Jersey Central Power and Light customers in northern Monmouth County lost power in the storm. The strong winds, coupled with the rain, had loosened up some of the tree branches, which then fell onto electrical equipment, according to the power company. The incident closed the Boardwalk from St. James Place to New York Avenue. A large tree came to rest on a building in the 200 Block of Mount Vernon in Northfield (Atlantic County) on the 12th. Strong winds toppled a signal light at Washington Avenue and Atlantic Avenue in Margate (Atlantic County) at about 6:30 AM EDT on the 12th. A 25-foot recreational boat docked behind a home near 18th Street in Ocean City was swamped and sunk. Schools in the Wildwoods closed and Ocean City dismissed early to avoid the worst of the flooding. An Upper Township woman who tried to drive from Strathmere through Sea Isle City became trapped at about 3:00 PM EDT on the 12th after her car stalled near 23rd Street and Landis Avenue. She was rescued by boat without incident. Lower Township Police reported multiple wires, utility poles and trees down. Several houses sustained structural damage when trees fell onto them. A live electrical line came down on a Lower Township school bus but there were no children on board, and the driver was able to get away without being injured. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system in the Ohio Valley during the early morning hours of May 11th moved eastward and gradually weakened. A secondary low formed over northeastern North Carolina during the late afternoon on the 11th and eventually absorbed the other low pressure system, becoming the dominant storm. This storm gradually moved east northeastward late on the 11th into the 12th. Early on the morning of the 12th, the storm was centered over the southern Delmarva. As this storm strengthened fairly rapidly, a strong northeast wind developed and persisted through the evening of the 12th. The prolonged northeasterly flow combined with higher than normal tides, caused moderate tidal flooding and areas of beach erosion in New Jersey. The heaviest rain remained across Delaware and Maryland. By the evening of the 12th, the coastal storm tracked eastward and gradually moved away from the region.||The Nor���easter of May 12th downed trees and wires, and flooded streets in dozens of beach towns. Tidal flooding forced the closure of schools and roads in parts of coastal New Jersey on the 12th and 13th. In Stafford (Ocean County), a truck that was buffeted by the winds got stuck on the Route 72 causeway bridge and blocked traffic. In Ocean County, widespread problems were reported from Stafford Township to Bass River Township due to downed power lines and trees on the 12th, most of which were considered nuisances and annoyances. Minor flooding, which in some cases resulted in road closures were reported. High winds caused the Route 72 Bridge to be closed for an extended period of time late in the morning on the 12th. The bridge was reopened shortly after 4:00 PM EDT on the 12th. Across the island, high winds brought down utility lines and sent debris flying. A billboard for a local ReMax real estate office on East Bay Avenue in Stafford Township was shredded on the 12th due to winds topping nearly 70 mph. In Barnegat Township (Ocean County), nearly 20 trees in various spots of town were knocked down, however there were no power outages or injuries reported. Bayshore Drive was shut down as bay water inundated it in the portion by the public beach and the municipal dock. A tree, which fell on Lower Shore Road, closed that road near the recreation complex. Bay Beach and the municipal dock parking lot were under a foot-and-a-half of water. Some of the older homes reportedly lost some roof shingles as one resident reported them laying in their yard. The strong winds even blew the shopping carts from the Genuardi's supermarket onto West Bay Avenue. ||In Surf City (Ocean County), the Causeway Bridge was closed by authorities from midday to about 5:00 PM EDT on the 12th when the winds approached 65 mph. A truck actually got stuck on the bridge as the driver could not control the drift of the vehicle. The flood-prone and low-lying Tuckerton Beach (Ocean County) experienced increased tides due to the coastal storm, which flooded some areas, and at least two cars were lost. During the morning of the 12th, residents awoke to three feet of water around their homes. There were portable toilets on their sides and floating in the middle of the street, and numerous 20-pound propane tanks also floated around. The strong winds also brought down a couple of power lines and blew a boat off of a boatlift. Some homes lost their siding while others had lost roof shingles. In Beach Haven (Ocean County), the high tide and strong onshore winds allowed water to breach over the street entrance off of Merivale and Nelsen Avenues. On the Bayfront, the water had cleared over some of the bulkheads at Taylor Avenue. The usual flooding occurred along some of the side streets off of Bay Avenue. In Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), extra sand was brought in to fill in beach entrances from Passaic Avenue southward. Severe winds damaged houses throughout the borough, with three separate reports of windows being blown open and one house had siding torn off. The bridges to Long Beach Island (Ocean County) were closed shortly before 11:00 AM EDT on the 12th when a box truck was immobilized in high wind near the crest of the span. At approximately 2:30 PM EDT on the 12th, the wind speed was 67.9 mph on the bridge, according to officials. Also, a section of Long Beach Boulevard was closed due to flooding that reached heights of one and a half feet in some spots. Route 72 was blocked off at Marsha Drive near Beach Haven West on the 12th when the causeway bridge connecting Stafford to Long Beach Island (Ocean County) was shut down because of strong winds. ||The Nor'easter of May 11-12 uprooted a few large trees in the Manasquan area (Monmouth County). Many area power lines were downed, which caused a number of power outages. Waves crashed over the inlet promenade at Riverside Drive on the afternoon of the 12th, which spilled onto the roadway. A large tree limb snapped off and fell onto a home in the Shark River Hills section of Neptune (Monmouth County) on the 12th. Damage to the dune fencing and even some poles were washed up on the beach. The vertical cut was generally up to 3 feet, however it reached up to 7 feet in Ocean City, with the horizontal cut averaging 2 to 5 feet. In Strathmere, about 80 percent of the emergency beachfill near Seaview was lost. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system in the Ohio Valley during the early morning hours of May 11th moved eastward and gradually weakened. A secondary low formed over northeastern North Carolina during the late afternoon on the 11th and eventually absorbed the other low pressure system, becoming the dominant storm. This storm gradually moved east northeastward late on the 11th into the 12th. Early on the morning of the 12th, the storm was centered over the southern Delmarva. As this storm strengthened fairly rapidly, a strong northeast wind developed and persisted through the evening of the 12th. The prolonged northeasterly flow combined with higher than normal tides, caused moderate tidal flooding and areas of beach erosion in New Jersey. The heaviest rain remained across Delaware and Maryland. By the evening of the 12th, the coastal storm tracked eastward and gradually moved away from the region.||The Nor���easter of May 12th downed trees and wires, and flooded streets in dozens of beach towns. Tidal flooding forced the closure of schools and roads in parts of coastal New Jersey on the 12th and 13th. In Stafford (Ocean County), a truck that was buffeted by the winds got stuck on the Route 72 causeway bridge and blocked traffic. In Ocean County, widespread problems were reported from Stafford Township to Bass River Township due to downed power lines and trees on the 12th, most of which were considered nuisances and annoyances. Minor flooding, which in some cases resulted in road closures were reported. High winds caused the Route 72 Bridge to be closed for an extended period of time late in the morning on the 12th. The bridge was reopened shortly after 4:00 PM EDT on the 12th. Across the island, high winds brought down utility lines and sent debris flying. A billboard for a local ReMax real estate office on East Bay Avenue in Stafford Township was shredded on the 12th due to winds topping nearly 70 mph. In Barnegat Township (Ocean County), nearly 20 trees in various spots of town were knocked down, however there were no power outages or injuries reported. Bayshore Drive was shut down as bay water inundated it in the portion by the public beach and the municipal dock. A tree, which fell on Lower Shore Road, closed that road near the recreation complex. Bay Beach and the municipal dock parking lot were under a foot-and-a-half of water. Some of the older homes reportedly lost some roof shingles as one resident reported them laying in their yard. The strong winds even blew the shopping carts from the Genuardi's supermarket onto West Bay Avenue. ||In Surf City (Ocean County), the Causeway Bridge was closed by authorities from midday to about 5:00 PM EDT on the 12th when the winds approached 65 mph. A truck actually got stuck on the bridge as the driver could not control the drift of the vehicle. The flood-prone and low-lying Tuckerton Beach (Ocean County) experienced increased tides due to the coastal storm, which flooded some areas, and at least two cars were lost. During the morning of the 12th, residents awoke to three feet of water around their homes. There were portable toilets on their sides and floating in the middle of the street, and numerous 20-pound propane tanks also floated around. The strong winds also brought down a couple of power lines and blew a boat off of a boatlift. Some homes lost their siding while others had lost roof shingles. In Beach Haven (Ocean County), the high tide and strong onshore winds allowed water to breach over the street entrance off of Merivale and Nelsen Avenues. On the Bayfront, the water had cleared over some of the bulkheads at Taylor Avenue. The usual flooding occurred along some of the side streets off of Bay Avenue. In Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), extra sand was brought in to fill in beach entrances from Passaic Avenue southward. Severe winds damaged houses throughout the borough, with three separate reports of windows being blown open and one house had siding torn off. The bridges to Long Beach Island (Ocean County) were closed shortly before 11:00 AM EDT on the 12th when a box truck was immobilized in high wind near the crest of the span. At approximately 2:30 PM EDT on the 12th, the wind speed was 67.9 mph on the bridge, according to officials. Also, a section of Long Beach Boulevard was closed due to flooding that reached heights of one and a half feet in some spots. Route 72 was blocked off at Marsha Drive near Beach Haven West on the 12th when the causeway bridge connecting Stafford to Long Beach Island (Ocean County) was shut down because of strong winds. ||The Nor'easter of May 11-12 uprooted a few large trees in the Manasquan area (Monmouth County). Also, tree limbs and branches, as well as trash and recycling, were scattered and strewn around the streets. Sand from the local beaches was also swept up over the boardwalks and deposited onto Ocean Avenue, and other beach roads, as a result of the strong winds. Many area power lines were downed, which caused a number of power outages. Waves crashed over the inlet promenade at Riverside Drive on the afternoon of the 12th, which spilled onto the roadway. The combination of the waves and high winds led trash receptacles along a walkway to take flight. The length of Brielle Road was blocked off as more than a foot of water covered it. Main Street was closed between Potter Avenue and Third Avenue due to flooding. A downed power line over Stockton Lake Boulevard, along with flood conditions from the overflowing lake, caused much of the length of that street to be closed. Manasquan's deputy Office of Emergency Management coordinator reported that a storm surge of 3.5 feet came ashore in the borough.||In Belmar (Monmouth County), many large trees, tree branches and tree limbs had fallen due to the high winds. A handful of utility poles throughout the borough were also damaged from the storm. On the afternoon of the 12th, the ocean's waters reached up to the borough's boardwalk at high tide. A large tree limb snapped off and fell onto a home in the Shark River Hills section of Neptune (Monmouth County) on the 12th. In Bradley Beach (Monmouth County), beachfront was hit with severe beach erosion along Ocean Avenue, with a lot of sand that blew up onto the roadways and boardwalk. The storm caused minor damage to the boardwalk gazebos, with shingles being blown away. Large amounts of sand, some to the depth of two feet, in Sea Girt (Monmouth County) was blown onto the boardwalk and Ocean Avenue. A weather station at Sea Girt's beachfront pavilion measured sustained winds of 60 to 64 mph between 9:30 AM EDT and 10:00 AM EDT on the 12th.||Wall Township (Monmouth County) received several reports of downed trees, and also some debris in the roads along with traffic lights that were not working. Brielle Borough experienced numerous downed wires and utility poles, several downed trees and moderate tidal flooding. The borough of Spring Lake (Monmouth County) and its boardwalk as well as much of Ocean Avenue, was inundated with sand blown in from the beach. Some downed trees were also reported with one downed tree near the intersection of Third and St. Claire avenues, which caused the road to be temporarily closed. Avon-by-the-sea beaches had sand pushed back towards the boardwalk, and crews used front-end loaders to try and spread the sand across the beachfront after the storm passed. Sea Bright (Monmouth County) experienced flooding on the 12th as strong winds and high tides occurred. Approximately 3,000 Jersey Central Power and Light customers in northern Monmouth County lost power in the storm. The strong winds, coupled with the rain, had loosened up some of the tree branches, which then fell onto electrical equipment, according to the power company. Ocean Avenue in front of a deli store became flooded with about four feet of water, while areas of the store had two feet of water.||Adams Avenue in Margate (Atlantic County) along with sections of Bay and New York Avenues in Somers Point (Atlantic County) were flooded due to high tides and a strong onshore wind associated with the Mother���s Day Nor���easter. In Strathmere, about 80 percent of the emergency beachfill near Seaview was lost. Also, the corner of the Seaview roadway was partially undermined due to the loss of sand, and the bulkhead was completely exposed. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tidal flooding was widespread minor to locally moderate in Atlantic County. The tidal gage at Atlantic City peaked at 7.4 feet mean lower low water at 1:00 AM EDT on the 12th. This is just below moderate, however moderate coastal flooding during high tide was reported. In addition, the Atlantic City Marina gage (back bay) peaked at 8.7 feet mean lower low water at 1:00 AM EDT on the 12th. Moderate flooding begins at 7.7 feet mean lower low water, except at the Atlantic City Marina gage where there is no established flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system in the Ohio Valley during the early morning hours of May 11th moved eastward and gradually weakened. A secondary low formed over northeastern North Carolina during the late afternoon on the 11th and eventually absorbed the other low pressure system, becoming the dominant storm. This storm gradually moved east northeastward late on the 11th into the 12th. Early on the morning of the 12th, the storm was centered over the southern Delmarva. As this storm strengthened fairly rapidly, a strong northeast wind developed and persisted through the evening of the 12th. The prolonged northeasterly flow combined with higher than normal tides, caused moderate tidal flooding and areas of beach erosion in New Jersey. The heaviest rain remained across Delaware and Maryland. By the evening of the 12th, the coastal storm tracked eastward and gradually moved away from the region.||The Nor���easter of May 12th downed trees and wires, and flooded streets in dozens of beach towns. Tidal flooding forced the closure of schools and roads in parts of coastal New Jersey on the 12th and 13th. In Stafford (Ocean County), a truck that was buffeted by the winds got stuck on the Route 72 causeway bridge and blocked traffic. In Ocean County, widespread problems were reported from Stafford Township to Bass River Township due to downed power lines and trees on the 12th, most of which were considered nuisances and annoyances. Minor flooding, which in some cases resulted in road closures were reported. High winds caused the Route 72 Bridge to be closed for an extended period of time late in the morning on the 12th. The bridge was reopened shortly after 4:00 PM EDT on the 12th. Across the island, high winds brought down utility lines and sent debris flying. A billboard for a local ReMax real estate office on East Bay Avenue in Stafford Township was shredded on the 12th due to winds topping nearly 70 mph. In Barnegat Township (Ocean County), nearly 20 trees in various spots of town were knocked down, however there were no power outages or injuries reported. Bayshore Drive was shut down as bay water inundated it in the portion by the public beach and the municipal dock. A tree, which fell on Lower Shore Road, closed that road near the recreation complex. Bay Beach and the municipal dock parking lot were under a foot-and-a-half of water. Some of the older homes reportedly lost some roof shingles as one resident reported them laying in their yard. The strong winds even blew the shopping carts from the Genuardi's supermarket onto West Bay Avenue. ||In Surf City (Ocean County), the Causeway Bridge was closed by authorities from midday to about 5:00 PM EDT on the 12th when the winds approached 65 mph. A truck actually got stuck on the bridge as the driver could not control the drift of the vehicle. The flood-prone and low-lying Tuckerton Beach (Ocean County) experienced increased tides due to the coastal storm, which flooded some areas, and at least two cars were lost. During the morning of the 12th, residents awoke to three feet of water around their homes. There were portable toilets on their sides and floating in the middle of the street, and numerous 20-pound propane tanks also floated around. The strong winds also brought down a couple of power lines and blew a boat off of a boatlift. Some homes lost their siding while others had lost roof shingles. In Beach Haven (Ocean County), the high tide and strong onshore winds allowed water to breach over the street entrance off of Merivale and Nelsen Avenues. On the Bayfront, the water had cleared over some of the bulkheads at Taylor Avenue. The usual flooding occurred along some of the side streets off of Bay Avenue. In Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), extra sand was brought in to fill in beach entrances from Passaic Avenue southward. Severe winds damaged houses throughout the borough, with three separate reports of windows being blown open and one house had siding torn off. The bridges to Long Beach Island (Ocean County) were closed shortly before 11:00 AM EDT on the 12th when a box truck was immobilized in high wind near the crest of the span. At approximately 2:30 PM EDT on the 12th, the wind speed was 67.9 mph on the bridge, according to officials. Also, a section of Long Beach Boulevard was closed due to flooding that reached heights of one and a half feet in some spots. Route 72 was blocked off at Marsha Drive near Beach Haven West on the 12th when the causeway bridge connecting Stafford to Long Beach Island (Ocean County) was shut down because of strong winds. ||The Nor'easter of May 11-12 uprooted a few large trees in the Manasquan area (Monmouth County). Also, tree limbs and branches, as well as trash and recycling, were scattered and strewn around the streets. Sand from the local beaches was also swept up over the boardwalks and deposited onto Ocean Avenue, and other beach roads, as a result of the strong winds. Many area power lines were downed, which caused a number of power outages. Waves crashed over the inlet promenade at Riverside Drive on the afternoon of the 12th, which spilled onto the roadway. The combination of the waves and high winds led trash receptacles along a walkway to take flight. The length of Brielle Road was blocked off as more than a foot of water covered it. Main Street was closed between Potter Avenue and Third Avenue due to flooding. A downed power line over Stockton Lake Boulevard, along with flood conditions from the overflowing lake, caused much of the length of that street to be closed. Manasquan's deputy Office of Emergency Management coordinator reported that a storm surge of 3.5 feet came ashore in the borough.||In Belmar (Monmouth County), many large trees, tree branches and tree limbs had fallen due to the high winds. A large tree limb snapped off and fell onto a home in the Shark River Hills section of Neptune (Monmouth County) on the 12th. In Bradley Beach (Monmouth County), beachfront was hit with severe beach erosion along Ocean Avenue, with a lot of sand that blew up onto the roadways and boardwalk. The storm caused minor damage to the boardwalk gazebos, with shingles being blown away. Large amounts of sand, some to the depth of two feet, in Sea Girt (Monmouth County) was blown onto the boardwalk and Ocean Avenue. A weather station at Sea Girt's beachfront pavilion measured sustained winds of 60 to 64 mph between 9:30 AM EDT and 10:00 AM EDT on the 12th.||Wall Township (Monmouth County) received several reports of downed trees, and also some debris in the roads along with traffic lights that were not working. Brielle Borough experienced numerous downed wires and utility poles, several downed trees and moderate tidal flooding. The borough of Spring Lake (Monmouth County) and its boardwalk as well as much of Ocean Avenue, was inundated with sand blown in from the beach. Some downed trees were also reported with one downed tree near the intersection of Third and St. Claire avenues, which caused the road to be temporarily closed. Avon-by-the-sea beaches had sand pushed back towards the boardwalk, and crews used front-end loaders to try and spread the sand across the beachfront after the storm passed. Sea Bright (Monmouth County) experienced flooding on the 12th as strong winds and high tides occurred. Approximately 3,000 Jersey Central Power and Light customers in northern Monmouth County lost power in the storm. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tidal flooding was widespread along the New Jersey Shore, in the minor to moderate range. The tidal gage at the Cape May Ferry Terminal peaked at 8.1 feet mean lower low water at 2:00 AM on the 12th. Moderate flooding begins at 7.7 feet mean lower low water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system in the Ohio Valley during the early morning hours of May 11th moved eastward and gradually weakened. A secondary low formed over northeastern North Carolina during the late afternoon on the 11th and eventually absorbed the other low pressure system, becoming the dominant storm. This storm gradually moved east northeastward late on the 11th into the 12th. Early on the morning of the 12th, the storm was centered over the southern Delmarva. As this storm strengthened fairly rapidly, a strong northeast wind developed and persisted through the evening of the 12th. The prolonged northeast flow combined with higher than normal tides, which caused tidal flooding in New Jersey along the Delaware River and Delaware Bay. There was limited moisture inland from the center of the storm. By the evening of the 12th, the coastal storm tracked eastward and gradually moved away from the region.||A number of Cumberland County residents remained without power on the 13th following high winds and heavy rains on the 12th. Atlantic City Electric reported on the 13th that crews continued to work to restore power to some 200 customers who lost power during the storm. The majority of the outages on the 12th were caused by downed trees and tree limbs. Utilities reported more than 60,000 customers were without power in New Jersey. A large portion of a tree limb fell onto the roof of a home in Upper Deerfield (Cumberland County) on the 13th after heavy rain and strong winds compromised the trunk. There were numerous reports of tree limbs and other debris on Salem County roadways. The Red Cross set up a shelter for Pennsville (Salem County). In Bridgeton (Cumberland County), eight reports of downed trees or large limbs blocking roadways were received between 1:00 AM EDT and 1:00 PM EDT on the 12th, with the bulk of that activity having occurred between 10:00 AM EDT and 11:00 AM EDT. Several trees were downed in Vineland (Cumberland County), with a couple which fell across public streets and one tree hit a house. The home suffered considerable damage as the front of the structure was torn off. The Department of Streets in Millville (Cumberland County) reported that six streets were blocked by fallen trees. In Cumberland County, a wind gust to 64 mph was reported in Fortescue during the night of the 11th and early morning of the 12th. ||An east wind drove in the tide, which filled marshes with water, into parts of coastal Cumberland County. Roads into Gandy's Beach and Money Island were impassable, as they were covered with debris and with two feet of sand over the road in at least one place. As a result, the sand needed to be bulldozed away. Floating propane tanks also presented a problem in Downe (Cumberland County). Five or six were recovered floating in the Gandy's Beach-Money Island area and another half-dozen in Fortescue (Cumberland County). At 2:00 PM EDT on the 12th, 3,900 Atlantic City Electric customers were without power across Cumberland County. By 9:15 PM EDT on the 12th, that number had dropped considerably. ||In Salem County, reports started to arrive into the 911 Center around 2:30 AM EDT on the 12th regarding downed trees and debris blocking several roads. The following roads were closed due to flooding in Gloucester County; Route 322 at Route 130 southbound (Logan Township); Route 130 at High Hill Road (Logan Township); Center Square Road between Pedricktown Road and Route 130 (Logan Township); Route 44 at Crown Point Road (West Deptford Township). There was a report of a levee failure in Logan Township, and local resources were sent with concrete rip-raf to strengthen up the levee. In Burlington County, some trees were either snapped off or completely uprooted due to the rain and strong winds on the 12th, including in the Moorestown and Southampton areas. The New Jersey Turnpike restricted motorcycles and cars pulling trailers from using the highway on the 12th due to the strong winds.||Some peak wind gusts included; 57 mph in Tabernacle; 51 mph in Chatsworth Coyle Field; 50 mph in Bivalve; 48 mph at the Millville Airport; and 46 mph at McGuire Air Force Base.||Minor tidal flooding occurred along the Delaware River in Gloucester and Camden Counties as the Philadelphia tide gage peaked at 8.9 feet mean lower low water at 8:00 AM EDT on the 12th. Moderate tidal flooding was observered in Salem and Cumberland Counties. The Reedy Point, Delaware tidal gage reached 8.3 feet mean lower low water at 6:00 AM EDT on the 12th (moderate flooding begins at 8.2 feet mean lower low water). The Cape May Ferry Terminal tide gage, which is used for Cumberland County, reached 8.1 feet mean lower low water at 2:00 AM EDT on the 12th (moderate flooding begins at 7.7 feet mean lower low water). A newer tide gage at Delaware City, Delaware reached 9.1 feet mean lower low water at 6:00 AM EDT on the 12th. This was reported to be a record level since the gage was installed in 2001.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate tidal flooding was observered in Salem County along the upper Delaware Bay and lower Delaware River. The Reedy Point, Delaware tidal gage reached 8.3 feet mean lower low water at 6:00 AM EDT on the 12th (moderate flooding begins at 8.2 feet mean lower low water). A newer tide gage at Delaware City, Delaware reached 9.1 feet mean lower low water at 6:00 AM EDT on the 12th. This was reported to be a record level since the gage was installed in 2001.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system in the Ohio Valley during the early morning hours of May 11th moved eastward and gradually weakened. A secondary low formed over northeastern North Carolina during the late afternoon on the 11th and eventually absorbed the other low pressure system, becoming the dominant storm. This storm gradually moved east northeastward late on the 11th into the 12th. Early on the morning of the 12th, the storm was centered over the southern Delmarva. As this storm strengthened fairly rapidly, a strong northeast wind developed and persisted through the evening of the 12th. The prolonged northeasterly flow combined with higher than normal tides, caused moderate tidal flooding and areas of beach erosion in New Jersey. The heaviest rain remained across Delaware and Maryland. By the evening of the 12th, the coastal storm tracked eastward and gradually moved away from the region.||The Nor���easter of May 12th downed trees and wires, and flooded streets in dozens of beach towns. Tidal flooding forced the closure of schools and roads in parts of coastal New Jersey on the 12th and 13th. In Stafford (Ocean County), a truck that was buffeted by the winds got stuck on the Route 72 causeway bridge and blocked traffic. In Ocean County, widespread problems were reported from Stafford Township to Bass River Township due to downed power lines and trees on the 12th, most of which were considered nuisances and annoyances. Minor flooding, which in some cases resulted in road closures were reported. High winds caused the Route 72 Bridge to be closed for an extended period of time late in the morning on the 12th. The bridge was reopened shortly after 4:00 PM EDT on the 12th. Across the island, high winds brought down utility lines and sent debris flying. A billboard for a local ReMax real estate office on East Bay Avenue in Stafford Township was shredded on the 12th due to winds topping nearly 70 mph. In Barnegat Township (Ocean County), nearly 20 trees in various spots of town were knocked down, however there were no power outages or injuries reported. Bayshore Drive was shut down as bay water inundated it in the portion by the public beach and the municipal dock. A tree, which fell on Lower Shore Road, closed that road near the recreation complex. Bay Beach and the municipal dock parking lot were under a foot-and-a-half of water. Some of the older homes reportedly lost some roof shingles as one resident reported them laying in their yard. The strong winds even blew the shopping carts from the Genuardi's supermarket onto West Bay Avenue. ||In Surf City (Ocean County), the Causeway Bridge was closed by authorities from midday to about 5:00 PM EDT on the 12th when the winds approached 65 mph. A truck actually got stuck on the bridge as the driver could not control the drift of the vehicle. The flood-prone and low-lying Tuckerton Beach (Ocean County) experienced increased tides due to the coastal storm, which flooded some areas, and at least two cars were lost. During the morning of the 12th, residents awoke to three feet of water around their homes. There were portable toilets on their sides and floating in the middle of the street, and numerous 20-pound propane tanks also floated around. The strong winds also brought down a couple of power lines and blew a boat off of a boatlift. Some homes lost their siding while others had lost roof shingles. In Beach Haven (Ocean County), the high tide and strong onshore winds allowed water to breach over the street entrance off of Merivale and Nelsen Avenues. On the Bayfront, the water had cleared over some of the bulkheads at Taylor Avenue. The usual flooding occurred along some of the side streets off of Bay Avenue. In Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), extra sand was brought in to fill in beach entrances from Passaic Avenue southward. Severe winds damaged houses throughout the borough, with three separate reports of windows being blown open and one house had siding torn off. The bridges to Long Beach Island (Ocean County) were closed shortly before 11:00 AM EDT on the 12th when a box truck was immobilized in high wind near the crest of the span. At approximately 2:30 PM EDT on the 12th, the wind speed was 67.9 mph on the bridge, according to officials. Also, a section of Long Beach Boulevard was closed due to flooding that reached heights of one and a half feet in some spots. Route 72 was blocked off at Marsha Drive near Beach Haven West on the 12th when the causeway bridge connecting Stafford to Long Beach Island (Ocean County) was shut down because of strong winds. ||The Nor'easter of May 11-12 uprooted a few large trees in the Manasquan area (Monmouth County). Also, tree limbs and branches, as well as trash and recycling, were scattered and strewn around the streets. Sand from the local beaches was also swept up over the boardwalks and deposited onto Ocean Avenue, and other beach roads, as a result of the strong winds. Many area power lines were downed, which caused a number of power outages. Waves crashed over the inlet promenade at Riverside Drive on the afternoon of the 12th, which spilled onto the roadway. The combination of the waves and high winds led trash receptacles along a walkway to take flight. The length of Brielle Road was blocked off as more than a foot of water covered it. Main Street was closed between Potter Avenue and Third Avenue due to flooding. A downed power line over Stockton Lake Boulevard, along with flood conditions from the overflowing lake, caused much of the length of that street to be closed. Manasquan's deputy Office of Emergency Management coordinator reported that a storm surge of 3.5 feet came ashore in the borough.||In Belmar (Monmouth County), many large trees, tree branches and tree limbs had fallen due to the high winds. A handful of utility poles throughout the borough were also damaged from the storm. On the afternoon of the 12th, the ocean's waters reached up to the borough's boardwalk at high tide. A large tree limb snapped off and fell onto a home in the Shark River Hills section of Neptune (Monmouth County) on the 12th. In Bradley Beach (Monmouth County), beachfront was hit with severe beach erosion along Ocean Avenue, with a lot of sand that blew up onto the roadways and boardwalk. The storm caused minor damage to the boardwalk gazebos, with shingles being blown away. Large amounts of sand, some to the depth of two feet, in Sea Girt (Monmouth County) was blown onto the boardwalk and Ocean Avenue. A weather station at Sea Girt's beachfront pavilion measured sustained winds of 60 to 64 mph between 9:30 AM EDT and 10:00 AM EDT on the 12th.||Wall Township (Monmouth County) received several reports of downed trees, and also some debris in the roads along with traffic lights that were not working. Brielle Borough experienced numerous downed wires and utility poles, several downed trees and moderate tidal flooding. The borough of Spring Lake (Monmouth County) and its boardwalk as well as much of Ocean Avenue, was inundated with sand blown in from the beach. Some downed trees were also reported with one downed tree near the intersection of Third and St. Claire avenues, which caused the road to be temporarily closed. Avon-by-the-sea beaches had sand pushed back towards the boardwalk, and crews used front-end loaders to try and spread the sand across the beachfront after the storm passed. Sea Bright (Monmouth County) experienced flooding on the 12th as strong winds and high tides occurred. Approximately 3,000 Jersey Central Power and Light customers in northern Monmouth County lost power in the storm. The strong winds, coupled with the rain, had loosened up some of the tree branches, which then fell onto electrical equipment, according to the power company. Ocean Avenue in front of a deli store became flooded with about four feet of water, while areas of the store had two feet of water.||Adams Avenue in Margate (Atlantic County) along with sections of Bay and New York Avenues in Somers Point (Atlantic County) were flooded due to high tides and a strong onshore wind associated with the Mother���s Day Nor���easter. A huge piece of sheet metal landed on the Atlantic City Boardwalk (Atlantic County) after it blew off an unknown location on the 12th. The incident closed the Boardwalk from St. James Place to New York Avenue. A large tree came to rest on a building in the 200 Block of Mount Vernon in Northfield (Atlantic County) on the 12th. Strong winds toppled a signal light at Washington Avenue and Atlantic Avenue in Margate (Atlantic County) at about 6:30 AM EDT on the 12th. Flooding occurred on New York Avenue on the 12th in Somers Point (Atlantic County). Flooding in Atlantic City closed down the White Horse and the Black Horse Pikes on the 12th. These reopened at 9:30 PM EDT and 10:00 PM EDT respectfully. In Atlantic City, a man who was walking on Albany Avenue near the edge of the city was hit by flying debris, but was not seriously injured. The city had to shut down sections of its Boardwalk to pedestrian traffic for part of the day after a 20-by-25-foot piece of metal blew down. East Shore Drive and 12th Street North in Brigantine (Atlantic County) was flooded on the 12th.||Streets, homes and property were flooded on the 12th from high tides and strong winds in Ocean City and Strathmere (Cape May County). Even bridges into Ocean City were closed for a time and portable toilets on construction sites floated away. Some motorists disregarded warnings and the flooding levels and had to be removed from their stranded vehicles. Some areas that received significant flooding included Third and Fourth Streets along Bay Avenue, 30th Street and 34th Street between West, Simpson and Haven Avenues. The city closed the Route 52 causeway at 3:00 PM EDT on the 12th, and closed it again from 3:00 PM to 9:00 PM EDT during high tides. The 34th Street Bridge into Upper Township (Cape May County) was open to traffic for only one lane. Law enforcement received numerous calls about downed utility wires and minor power outages. Trash and debris floated in neighborhood streets as the floodwaters rose during the afternoon high tide on the 12th. On West 17th Street, plastic portable toilets from neighboring construction sites floated down the road. Cape May County declared a limited state of emergency from May 12th to the 13th due to the Nor���easter. At 9:15 PM EDT, Atlantic City Electric reported 16,521 customers in Cape May County and nearly 9,100 customers in Atlantic County were without power.||Cape May County declared a state of emergency due to flooding at 2:00 PM EDT on the 12th. The flooding occurred on all the barrier islands, and people left on their own, but it was not mandatory. Schools were closed in Wildwood, North Wildwood and West Wildwood due to tidal flooding, and the Route 52 causeway between Ocean City and Somers Point had to be closed at 2:00 PM EDT on the 12th because of flooding. A 25-foot recreational boat docked behind a home near 18th Street in Ocean City was swamped and sunk. Schools in the Wildwoods closed and Ocean City dismissed early to avoid the worst of the flooding. An Upper Township woman who tried to drive from Strathmere through Sea Isle City became trapped at about 3:00 PM EDT on the 12th after her car stalled near 23rd Street and Landis Avenue. She was rescued by boat without incident. Lower Township Police reported multiple wires, utility poles and trees down. Several houses sustained structural damage when trees fell onto them. The North Wildwood Mayor reported downed wires with even some roofs removed from the strong winds. A live electrical line came down on a Lower Township school bus but there were no children on board, and the driver was able to get away without being injured. A section of dock at Dad's Place Marina on Ocean Drive in Middle Township was lifted and twisted apart on the 12th by high winds and waves. ||Bridges into Ocean City, Sea Isle City, Wildwood and Avalon (Cape May County) were closed on the 12th, and two of the three major routes into Atlantic City (Atlantic County) were also closed. Some schools were closed or dismissed early on the 12th. Atlantic City, the Wildwoods and Cape May County governments declared states of emergency. Atlantic Electric reported more than 45,000 of its New Jersey customers were without power.||Tidal flooding was widespread along the New Jersey Shore, in the minor to moderate range. The tidal gage at Sandy Hook peaked at 7.8 feet mean lower low water at 3:00 PM on the 12th; the Atlantic City gage peaked at 7.40 feet mean lower low water at 1:00 AM EDT on the 12th; the Atlantic City Marina gage (back bay) peaked at 8.7 feet mean lower low water at 1:00 AM EDT on the 12th; and the Cape May Ferry Terminal gage peaked at 8.1 feet mean lower low water at 2:00 AM on the 12th. Moderate flooding begins at 7.7 feet mean lower low water, except at the Atlantic City Marina gage where there is no established flood stage.||Some peak wind gusts included: 78 mph in Ocean City; 76 mph in Sea Isle City; 66 mph in Cape May (Coop) and Atlantic City (NJDOT station); 64 mph in Fortescue; 62 mph in Avalon and Harvey Cedars; 59 mph at Barnegat Light and at the Atlantic City International Airport in Pomona; 57 mph in Tabernacle; 56 mph in Hillsborough; 55 mph in Seaside Heights; 52 mph in Sea Girt; 50 mph Bivalve; 48 mph at the Millville Airport; 47 mph in Woodstown; 46 mph at the Trenton-Mercer County Airport; and 44 mph in Pittstown. Some rainfall totals included: 1.77 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport in Pomona; 1.22 inches in Franklin Township; 1.18 inches in Brick; 1.16 inches in Estell Manor; and 1.07 inches in Manasquan.||According to a New Jersey State coastal storm survey, the following impacts to the beaches was reported. ||In Monmouth County, a sloping cut of 2 to 4 feet high by 25 to 50 feet wide was evident with some spots to 5 feet high. The largest sloping cut was generally from Bradley Beach on southward where windblown sand was reported along with no reported damage. ||In Ocean County, a sloping cut of generally 3 to 4 feet high by 50 feet to 75 feet wide was evident. Several locations, which included, Barnegat Light, Loveladies, Long Beach Township, Brand Beach, Beach Haven to Holgate, had a 6 to 8 foot (locally 10 feet or more) vertical by 6 to 10 foot horizontal cut to the dune system. Damage to the dune fencing and even some poles were washed up on the beach. At Beach Haven, the temporary dune at Merivale was washed away, and waves overwashed onto the street to Long Beach Boulevard. In Holgate, there were sporadic cuts in the dune system throughout the town, which totaled approximately 600 linear feet. The groins were exposed at the dune toe in Bay Head. ||In Atlantic County, a sloping cut of 1 to 2 feet high by 25 feet to 50 feet wide was reported along with no reported damage. ||In Cape May County, a sloping cut of generally 1 to 3 feet high to locally 4 feet high by 25 feet to 75 feet wide was reported. The vertical cut was generally up to 3 feet, however it reached up to 7 feet in Ocean City, with the horizontal cut averaging 2 to 5 feet. In Strathmere, about 80 percent of the emergency beachfill near Seaview was lost. Also, the corner of the Seaview roadway was partially undermined due to the loss of sand, and the bulkhead was completely exposed. Damage was done to dune fencing in Sea Isle City and there was damage done to the access steps near the promenade. In Avalon, windblown sand was reported and the geotubes and groin were exposed from 14th Street to 17th Street. In the Wildwoods, there was some damage done to the fencing, and there was forebeach flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tidal flooding was widespread along the New Jersey Shore and along the coast of Delaware Bay, in the minor to moderate range. The tidal gage at the Cape May Ferry Terminal peaked at 8.1 feet mean lower low water at 2:00 AM on the 12th. Moderate flooding begins at 7.7 feet mean lower low water.

Some downed trees were also reported with one downed tree near the intersection of Third and St. Claire avenues, which caused the road to be temporarily closed. Avon-by-the-sea beaches had sand pushed back towards the boardwalk, and crews used front-end loaders to try and spread the sand across the beachfront after the storm passed. Sea Bright (Monmouth County) experienced flooding on the 12th as strong winds and high tides occurred. Approximately 3,000 Jersey Central Power and Light customers in northern Monmouth County lost power in the storm. The strong winds, coupled with the rain, had loosened up some of the tree branches, which then fell onto electrical equipment, according to the power company. Ocean Avenue in front of a deli store became flooded with about four feet of water, while areas of the store had two feet of water.||Adams Avenue in Margate (Atlantic County) along with sections of Bay and New York Avenues in Somers Point (Atlantic County) were flooded due to high tides and a strong onshore wind associated with the Mother���s Day Nor���easter. A huge piece of sheet metal landed on the Atlantic City Boardwalk (Atlantic County) after it blew off an unknown location on the 12th. The incident closed the Boardwalk from St. James Place to New York Avenue. A large tree came to rest on a building in the 200 Block of Mount Vernon in Northfield (Atlantic County) on the 12th. Strong winds toppled a signal light at Washington Avenue and Atlantic Avenue in Margate (Atlantic County) at about 6:30 AM EDT on the 12th. Flooding occurred on New York Avenue on the 12th in Somers Point (Atlantic County). Flooding in Atlantic City closed down the White Horse and the Black Horse Pikes on the 12th. These reopened at 9:30 PM EDT and 10:00 PM EDT respectfully. In Atlantic City, a man who was walking on Albany Avenue near the edge of the city was hit by flying debris, but was not seriously injured. The city had to shut down sections of its Boardwalk to pedestrian traffic for part of the day after a 20-by-25-foot piece of metal blew down. East Shore Drive and 12th Street North in Brigantine (Atlantic County) was flooded on the 12th.||Streets, homes and property were flooded on the 12th from high tides and strong winds in Ocean City and Strathmere (Cape May County). Even bridges into Ocean City were closed for a time and portable toilets on construction sites floated away. Some motorists disregarded warnings and the flooding levels and had to be removed from their stranded vehicles. Some areas that received significant flooding included Third and Fourth Streets along Bay Avenue, 30th Street and 34th Street between West, Simpson and Haven Avenues. The city closed the Route 52 causeway at 3:00 PM EDT on the 12th, and closed it again from 3:00 PM to 9:00 PM EDT during high tides. The 34th Street Bridge into Upper Township (Cape May County) was open to traffic for only one lane. Law enforcement received numerous calls about downed utility wires and minor power outages. Trash and debris floated in neighborhood streets as the floodwaters rose during the afternoon high tide on the 12th. On West 17th Street, plastic portable toilets from neighboring construction sites floated down the road. Cape May County declared a limited state of emergency from May 12th to the 13th due to the Nor���easter. At 9:15 PM EDT, Atlantic City Electric reported 16,521 customers in Cape May County and nearly 9,100 customers in Atlantic County were without power.||Cape May County declared a state of emergency due to flooding at 2:00 PM EDT on the 12th. The flooding occurred on all the barrier islands, and people left on their own, but it was not mandatory. Schools were closed in Wildwood, North Wildwood and West Wildwood due to tidal flooding, and the Route 52 causeway between Ocean City and Somers Point had to be closed at 2:00 PM EDT on the 12th because of flooding. A 25-foot recreational boat docked behind a home near 18th Street in Ocean City was swamped and sunk. Schools in the Wildwoods closed and Ocean City dismissed early to avoid the worst of the flooding. An Upper Township woman who tried to drive from Strathmere through Sea Isle City became trapped at about 3:00 PM EDT on the 12th after her car stalled near 23rd Street and Landis Avenue. She was rescued by boat without incident. Lower Township Police reported multiple wires, utility poles and trees down. Several houses sustained structural damage when trees fell onto them. The North Wildwood Mayor reported downed wires with even some roofs removed from the strong winds. A live electrical line came down on a Lower Township school bus but there were no children on board, and the driver was able to get away without being injured. A section of dock at Dad's Place Marina on Ocean Drive in Middle Township was lifted and twisted apart on the 12th by high winds and waves. ||Bridges into Ocean City, Sea Isle City, Wildwood and Avalon (Cape May County) were closed on the 12th, and two of the three major routes into Atlantic City (Atlantic County) were also closed. The tidal gage at Sandy Hook peaked at 7.8 feet mean lower low water at 3:00 PM on the 12th; the Atlantic City gage peaked at 7.40 feet mean lower low water at 1:00 AM EDT on the 12th; the Atlantic City Marina gage (back bay) peaked at 8.7 feet mean lower low water at 1:00 AM EDT on the 12th; and the Cape May Ferry Terminal gage peaked at 8.1 feet mean lower low water at 2:00 AM on the 12th. Moderate flooding begins at 7.7 feet mean lower low water, except at the Atlantic City Marina gage where there is no established flood stage.||Some peak wind gusts included: 78 mph in Ocean City; 76 mph in Sea Isle City; 66 mph in Cape May (Coop) and Atlantic City (NJDOT station); 64 mph in Fortescue; 62 mph in Avalon and Harvey Cedars; 59 mph at Barnegat Light and at the Atlantic City International Airport in Pomona; 57 mph in Tabernacle; 56 mph in Hillsborough; 55 mph in Seaside Heights; 52 mph in Sea Girt; 50 mph Bivalve; 48 mph at the Millville Airport; 47 mph in Woodstown; 46 mph at the Trenton-Mercer County Airport; and 44 mph in Pittstown. Several locations, which included, Barnegat Light, Loveladies, Long Beach Township, Brand Beach, Beach Haven to Holgate, had a 6 to 8 foot (locally 10 feet or more) vertical by 6 to 10 foot horizontal cut to the dune system. Damage to the dune fencing and even some poles were washed up on the beach. At Beach Haven, the temporary dune at Merivale was washed away, and waves overwashed onto the street to Long Beach Boulevard. In Holgate, there were sporadic cuts in the dune system throughout the town, which totaled approximately 600 linear feet. The vertical cut was generally up to 3 feet, however it reached up to 7 feet in Ocean City, with the horizontal cut averaging 2 to 5 feet. In Strathmere, about 80 percent of the emergency beachfill near Seaview was lost. Also, the corner of the Seaview roadway was partially undermined due to the loss of sand, and the bulkhead was completely exposed. Damage was done to dune fencing in Sea Isle City and there was damage done to the access steps near the promenade. In Avalon, windblown sand was reported and the geotubes and groin were exposed from 14th Street to 17th Street. In the Wildwoods, there was some damage done to the fencing, and there was forebeach flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak winds during the morning of the 12th included, 66 mph at a Atlantic City New Jersey Department of Transportation station and 59 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport in Pomona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system in the Ohio Valley during the early morning hours of May 11th moved eastward and gradually weakened. A secondary low formed over northeastern North Carolina during the late afternoon on the 11th and eventually absorbed the other low pressure system, becoming the dominant storm. This storm gradually moved east northeastward late on the 11th into the 12th. Early on the morning of the 12th, the storm was centered over the southern Delmarva. As this storm strengthened fairly rapidly, a strong northeast wind developed and persisted through the evening of the 12th. The prolonged northeasterly flow combined with higher than normal tides, caused moderate tidal flooding and areas of beach erosion in New Jersey. The heaviest rain remained across Delaware and Maryland. By the evening of the 12th, the coastal storm tracked eastward and gradually moved away from the region.||The Nor���easter of May 12th downed trees and wires, and flooded streets in dozens of beach towns. Tidal flooding forced the closure of schools and roads in parts of coastal New Jersey on the 12th and 13th. In Stafford (Ocean County), a truck that was buffeted by the winds got stuck on the Route 72 causeway bridge and blocked traffic. In Ocean County, widespread problems were reported from Stafford Township to Bass River Township due to downed power lines and trees on the 12th, most of which were considered nuisances and annoyances. Minor flooding, which in some cases resulted in road closures were reported. High winds caused the Route 72 Bridge to be closed for an extended period of time late in the morning on the 12th. The bridge was reopened shortly after 4:00 PM EDT on the 12th. Across the island, high winds brought down utility lines and sent debris flying. A billboard for a local ReMax real estate office on East Bay Avenue in Stafford Township was shredded on the 12th due to winds topping nearly 70 mph. In Barnegat Township (Ocean County), nearly 20 trees in various spots of town were knocked down, however there were no power outages or injuries reported. Bayshore Drive was shut down as bay water inundated it in the portion by the public beach and the municipal dock. A tree, which fell on Lower Shore Road, closed that road near the recreation complex. Bay Beach and the municipal dock parking lot were under a foot-and-a-half of water. Some of the older homes reportedly lost some roof shingles as one resident reported them laying in their yard. The strong winds even blew the shopping carts from the Genuardi's supermarket onto West Bay Avenue. ||In Surf City (Ocean County), the Causeway Bridge was closed by authorities from midday to about 5:00 PM EDT on the 12th when the winds approached 65 mph. A truck actually got stuck on the bridge as the driver could not control the drift of the vehicle. The flood-prone and low-lying Tuckerton Beach (Ocean County) experienced increased tides due to the coastal storm, which flooded some areas, and at least two cars were lost. During the morning of the 12th, residents awoke to three feet of water around their homes. There were portable toilets on their sides and floating in the middle of the street, and numerous 20-pound propane tanks also floated around. The strong winds also brought down a couple of power lines and blew a boat off of a boatlift. Some homes lost their siding while others had lost roof shingles. In Beach Haven (Ocean County), the high tide and strong onshore winds allowed water to breach over the street entrance off of Merivale and Nelsen Avenues. On the Bayfront, the water had cleared over some of the bulkheads at Taylor Avenue. The usual flooding occurred along some of the side streets off of Bay Avenue. In Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), extra sand was brought in to fill in beach entrances from Passaic Avenue southward. Severe winds damaged houses throughout the borough, with three separate reports of windows being blown open and one house had siding torn off. The bridges to Long Beach Island (Ocean County) were closed shortly before 11:00 AM EDT on the 12th when a box truck was immobilized in high wind near the crest of the span. At approximately 2:30 PM EDT on the 12th, the wind speed was 67.9 mph on the bridge, according to officials. Also, a section of Long Beach Boulevard was closed due to flooding that reached heights of one and a half feet in some spots. Route 72 was blocked off at Marsha Drive near Beach Haven West on the 12th when the causeway bridge connecting Stafford to Long Beach Island (Ocean County) was shut down because of strong winds. ||The Nor'easter of May 11-12 uprooted a few large trees in the Manasquan area (Monmouth County). East Shore Drive and 12th Street North in Brigantine (Atlantic County) was flooded on the 12th.||Streets, homes and property were flooded on the 12th from high tides and strong winds in Ocean City and Strathmere (Cape May County). Even bridges into Ocean City were closed for a time and portable toilets on construction sites floated away. Some areas that received significant flooding included Third and Fourth Streets along Bay Avenue, 30th Street and 34th Street between West, Simpson and Haven Avenues. The city closed the Route 52 causeway at 3:00 PM EDT on the 12th, and closed it again from 3:00 PM to 9:00 PM EDT during high tides. The 34th Street Bridge into Upper Township (Cape May County) was open to traffic for only one lane. Law enforcement received numerous calls about downed utility wires and minor power outages. Trash and debris floated in neighborhood streets as the floodwaters rose during the afternoon high tide on the 12th. On West 17th Street, plastic portable toilets from neighboring construction sites floated down the road. A 25-foot recreational boat docked behind a home near 18th Street in Ocean City was swamped and sunk. Schools in the Wildwoods closed and Ocean City dismissed early to avoid the worst of the flooding. An Upper Township woman who tried to drive from Strathmere through Sea Isle City became trapped at about 3:00 PM EDT on the 12th after her car stalled near 23rd Street and Landis Avenue. She was rescued by boat without incident. Lower Township Police reported multiple wires, utility poles and trees down. Several houses sustained structural damage when trees fell onto them. The North Wildwood Mayor reported downed wires with even some roofs removed from the strong winds. A live electrical line came down on a Lower Township school bus but there were no children on board, and the driver was able to get away without being injured. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 78 mph was reported in Ocean City right along the shore during the early morning hours of the 12th. This reported was relayed by the Broadcast Media which received the measurement from a viewer who has a weather station. There was also are reported of a measured gust to 76 mph at 10:00 AM EDT on the 12th at Sea Isle City. The Cape May Harbor had a wind gust to 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system in the Ohio Valley during the early morning hours of May 11th moved eastward and gradually weakened. A secondary low formed over northeastern North Carolina during the late afternoon on the 11th and eventually absorbed the other low pressure system, becoming the dominant storm. This storm gradually moved east northeastward late on the 11th into the 12th. Early on the morning of the 12th, the storm was centered over the southern Delmarva. As this storm strengthened fairly rapidly, a strong northeast wind developed and persisted through the evening of the 12th. The prolonged northeasterly flow combined with higher than normal tides, caused moderate tidal flooding and areas of beach erosion in New Jersey. The heaviest rain remained across Delaware and Maryland. By the evening of the 12th, the coastal storm tracked eastward and gradually moved away from the region.||The Nor���easter of May 12th downed trees and wires, and flooded streets in dozens of beach towns. Tidal flooding forced the closure of schools and roads in parts of coastal New Jersey on the 12th and 13th. In Stafford (Ocean County), a truck that was buffeted by the winds got stuck on the Route 72 causeway bridge and blocked traffic. In Ocean County, widespread problems were reported from Stafford Township to Bass River Township due to downed power lines and trees on the 12th, most of which were considered nuisances and annoyances. Minor flooding, which in some cases resulted in road closures were reported. High winds caused the Route 72 Bridge to be closed for an extended period of time late in the morning on the 12th. The bridge was reopened shortly after 4:00 PM EDT on the 12th. Across the island, high winds brought down utility lines and sent debris flying. A billboard for a local ReMax real estate office on East Bay Avenue in Stafford Township was shredded on the 12th due to winds topping nearly 70 mph. In Barnegat Township (Ocean County), nearly 20 trees in various spots of town were knocked down, however there were no power outages or injuries reported. Bayshore Drive was shut down as bay water inundated it in the portion by the public beach and the municipal dock. A tree, which fell on Lower Shore Road, closed that road near the recreation complex. Bay Beach and the municipal dock parking lot were under a foot-and-a-half of water. Some of the older homes reportedly lost some roof shingles as one resident reported them laying in their yard. The strong winds even blew the shopping carts from the Genuardi's supermarket onto West Bay Avenue. ||In Surf City (Ocean County), the Causeway Bridge was closed by authorities from midday to about 5:00 PM EDT on the 12th when the winds approached 65 mph. A truck actually got stuck on the bridge as the driver could not control the drift of the vehicle. The flood-prone and low-lying Tuckerton Beach (Ocean County) experienced increased tides due to the coastal storm, which flooded some areas, and at least two cars were lost. During the morning of the 12th, residents awoke to three feet of water around their homes. There were portable toilets on their sides and floating in the middle of the street, and numerous 20-pound propane tanks also floated around. The strong winds also brought down a couple of power lines and blew a boat off of a boatlift. Some homes lost their siding while others had lost roof shingles. In Beach Haven (Ocean County), the high tide and strong onshore winds allowed water to breach over the street entrance off of Merivale and Nelsen Avenues. On the Bayfront, the water had cleared over some of the bulkheads at Taylor Avenue. The usual flooding occurred along some of the side streets off of Bay Avenue. In Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), extra sand was brought in to fill in beach entrances from Passaic Avenue southward. Severe winds damaged houses throughout the borough, with three separate reports of windows being blown open and one house had siding torn off. The bridges to Long Beach Island (Ocean County) were closed shortly before 11:00 AM EDT on the 12th when a box truck was immobilized in high wind near the crest of the span. At approximately 2:30 PM EDT on the 12th, the wind speed was 67.9 mph on the bridge, according to officials. Also, a section of Long Beach Boulevard was closed due to flooding that reached heights of one and a half feet in some spots. Route 72 was blocked off at Marsha Drive near Beach Haven West on the 12th when the causeway bridge connecting Stafford to Long Beach Island (Ocean County) was shut down because of strong winds. ||The Nor'easter of May 11-12 uprooted a few large trees in the Manasquan area (Monmouth County).
