An Upper Township woman who tried to drive from Strathmere through Sea Isle City became trapped at about 3:00 PM EDT on the 12th after her car stalled near 23rd Street and Landis Avenue. She was rescued by boat without incident. Lower Township Police reported multiple wires, utility poles and trees down. Several houses sustained structural damage when trees fell onto them. The North Wildwood Mayor reported downed wires with even some roofs removed from the strong winds. A live electrical line came down on a Lower Township school bus but there were no children on board, and the driver was able to get away without being injured. A section of dock at Dad's Place Marina on Ocean Drive in Middle Township was lifted and twisted apart on the 12th by high winds and waves. ||Bridges into Ocean City, Sea Isle City, Wildwood and Avalon (Cape May County) were closed on the 12th, and two of the three major routes into Atlantic City (Atlantic County) were also closed. Some schools were closed or dismissed early on the 12th. Atlantic City, the Wildwoods and Cape May County governments declared states of emergency. Atlantic Electric reported more than 45,000 of its New Jersey customers were without power.||Tidal flooding was widespread along the New Jersey Shore, in the minor to moderate range. The tidal gage at Sandy Hook peaked at 7.8 feet mean lower low water at 3:00 PM on the 12th; the Atlantic City gage peaked at 7.40 feet mean lower low water at 1:00 AM EDT on the 12th; the Atlantic City Marina gage (back bay) peaked at 8.7 feet mean lower low water at 1:00 AM EDT on the 12th; and the Cape May Ferry Terminal gage peaked at 8.1 feet mean lower low water at 2:00 AM on the 12th. Moderate flooding begins at 7.7 feet mean lower low water, except at the Atlantic City Marina gage where there is no established flood stage.||Some peak wind gusts included: 78 mph in Ocean City; 76 mph in Sea Isle City; 66 mph in Cape May (Coop) and Atlantic City (NJDOT station); 64 mph in Fortescue; 62 mph in Avalon and Harvey Cedars; 59 mph at Barnegat Light and at the Atlantic City International Airport in Pomona; 57 mph in Tabernacle; 56 mph in Hillsborough; 55 mph in Seaside Heights; 52 mph in Sea Girt; 50 mph Bivalve; 48 mph at the Millville Airport; 47 mph in Woodstown; 46 mph at the Trenton-Mercer County Airport; and 44 mph in Pittstown. Some rainfall totals included: 1.77 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport in Pomona; 1.22 inches in Franklin Township; 1.18 inches in Brick; 1.16 inches in Estell Manor; and 1.07 inches in Manasquan.||According to a New Jersey State coastal storm survey, the following impacts to the beaches was reported. ||In Monmouth County, a sloping cut of 2 to 4 feet high by 25 to 50 feet wide was evident with some spots to 5 feet high. The largest sloping cut was generally from Bradley Beach on southward where windblown sand was reported along with no reported damage. ||In Ocean County, a sloping cut of generally 3 to 4 feet high by 50 feet to 75 feet wide was evident. Several locations, which included, Barnegat Light, Loveladies, Long Beach Township, Brand Beach, Beach Haven to Holgate, had a 6 to 8 foot (locally 10 feet or more) vertical by 6 to 10 foot horizontal cut to the dune system. Damage to the dune fencing and even some poles were washed up on the beach. At Beach Haven, the temporary dune at Merivale was washed away, and waves overwashed onto the street to Long Beach Boulevard. In Holgate, there were sporadic cuts in the dune system throughout the town, which totaled approximately 600 linear feet. The groins were exposed at the dune toe in Bay Head. ||In Atlantic County, a sloping cut of 1 to 2 feet high by 25 feet to 50 feet wide was reported along with no reported damage. ||In Cape May County, a sloping cut of generally 1 to 3 feet high to locally 4 feet high by 25 feet to 75 feet wide was reported. The vertical cut was generally up to 3 feet, however it reached up to 7 feet in Ocean City, with the horizontal cut averaging 2 to 5 feet. In Strathmere, about 80 percent of the emergency beachfill near Seaview was lost. Also, the corner of the Seaview roadway was partially undermined due to the loss of sand, and the bulkhead was completely exposed. Damage was done to dune fencing in Sea Isle City and there was damage done to the access steps near the promenade. In Avalon, windblown sand was reported and the geotubes and groin were exposed from 14th Street to 17th Street. In the Wildwoods, there was some damage done to the fencing, and there was forebeach flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust to 64 mph was reported in Fortesque, which occurred during the very early morning hours of the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system in the Ohio Valley during the early morning hours of May 11th moved eastward and gradually weakened. A secondary low formed over northeastern North Carolina during the late afternoon on the 11th and eventually absorbed the other low pressure system, becoming the dominant storm. This storm gradually moved east northeastward late on the 11th into the 12th. Early on the morning of the 12th, the storm was centered over the southern Delmarva. As this storm strengthened fairly rapidly, a strong northeast wind developed and persisted through the evening of the 12th. The prolonged northeast flow combined with higher than normal tides, which caused tidal flooding in New Jersey along the Delaware River and Delaware Bay. There was limited moisture inland from the center of the storm. By the evening of the 12th, the coastal storm tracked eastward and gradually moved away from the region.||A number of Cumberland County residents remained without power on the 13th following high winds and heavy rains on the 12th. Atlantic City Electric reported on the 13th that crews continued to work to restore power to some 200 customers who lost power during the storm. The majority of the outages on the 12th were caused by downed trees and tree limbs. Utilities reported more than 60,000 customers were without power in New Jersey. A large portion of a tree limb fell onto the roof of a home in Upper Deerfield (Cumberland County) on the 13th after heavy rain and strong winds compromised the trunk. There were numerous reports of tree limbs and other debris on Salem County roadways. The Red Cross set up a shelter for Pennsville (Salem County). In Bridgeton (Cumberland County), eight reports of downed trees or large limbs blocking roadways were received between 1:00 AM EDT and 1:00 PM EDT on the 12th, with the bulk of that activity having occurred between 10:00 AM EDT and 11:00 AM EDT. Several trees were downed in Vineland (Cumberland County), with a couple which fell across public streets and one tree hit a house. The home suffered considerable damage as the front of the structure was torn off. The Department of Streets in Millville (Cumberland County) reported that six streets were blocked by fallen trees. In Cumberland County, a wind gust to 64 mph was reported in Fortescue during the night of the 11th and early morning of the 12th. ||An east wind drove in the tide, which filled marshes with water, into parts of coastal Cumberland County. Roads into Gandy's Beach and Money Island were impassable, as they were covered with debris and with two feet of sand over the road in at least one place. As a result, the sand needed to be bulldozed away. Floating propane tanks also presented a problem in Downe (Cumberland County). Five or six were recovered floating in the Gandy's Beach-Money Island area and another half-dozen in Fortescue (Cumberland County). At 2:00 PM EDT on the 12th, 3,900 Atlantic City Electric customers were without power across Cumberland County. By 9:15 PM EDT on the 12th, that number had dropped considerably. ||In Salem County, reports started to arrive into the 911 Center around 2:30 AM EDT on the 12th regarding downed trees and debris blocking several roads. The following roads were closed due to flooding in Gloucester County; Route 322 at Route 130 southbound (Logan Township); Route 130 at High Hill Road (Logan Township); Center Square Road between Pedricktown Road and Route 130 (Logan Township); Route 44 at Crown Point Road (West Deptford Township). There was a report of a levee failure in Logan Township, and local resources were sent with concrete rip-raf to strengthen up the levee. In Burlington County, some trees were either snapped off or completely uprooted due to the rain and strong winds on the 12th, including in the Moorestown and Southampton areas. The New Jersey Turnpike restricted motorcycles and cars pulling trailers from using the highway on the 12th due to the strong winds.||Some peak wind gusts included; 57 mph in Tabernacle; 51 mph in Chatsworth Coyle Field; 50 mph in Bivalve; 48 mph at the Millville Airport; and 46 mph at McGuire Air Force Base.||Minor tidal flooding occurred along the Delaware River in Gloucester and Camden Counties as the Philadelphia tide gage peaked at 8.9 feet mean lower low water at 8:00 AM EDT on the 12th. Moderate tidal flooding was observered in Salem and Cumberland Counties. The Reedy Point, Delaware tidal gage reached 8.3 feet mean lower low water at 6:00 AM EDT on the 12th (moderate flooding begins at 8.2 feet mean lower low water). The Cape May Ferry Terminal tide gage, which is used for Cumberland County, reached 8.1 feet mean lower low water at 2:00 AM EDT on the 12th (moderate flooding begins at 7.7 feet mean lower low water). A newer tide gage at Delaware City, Delaware reached 9.1 feet mean lower low water at 6:00 AM EDT on the 12th. This was reported to be a record level since the gage was installed in 2001.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported a wind gust of 57 mph in Tabernacle, which occurred around 11:50 AM EDT on the 12th. Other peak wind gusts included; 51 mph at Chatsworth Coyle Field; and 46 mph at McGuire Air Force Base. These gusts knocked down some trees and power lines in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system in the Ohio Valley during the early morning hours of May 11th moved eastward and gradually weakened. A secondary low formed over northeastern North Carolina during the late afternoon on the 11th and eventually absorbed the other low pressure system, becoming the dominant storm. This storm gradually moved east northeastward late on the 11th into the 12th. Early on the morning of the 12th, the storm was centered over the southern Delmarva. As this storm strengthened fairly rapidly, a strong northeast wind developed and persisted through the evening of the 12th. The prolonged northeasterly flow combined with higher than normal tides, caused moderate tidal flooding and areas of beach erosion in New Jersey. The heaviest rain remained across Delaware and Maryland. By the evening of the 12th, the coastal storm tracked eastward and gradually moved away from the region.||The Nor���easter of May 12th downed trees and wires, and flooded streets in dozens of beach towns. Tidal flooding forced the closure of schools and roads in parts of coastal New Jersey on the 12th and 13th. In Stafford (Ocean County), a truck that was buffeted by the winds got stuck on the Route 72 causeway bridge and blocked traffic. In Ocean County, widespread problems were reported from Stafford Township to Bass River Township due to downed power lines and trees on the 12th, most of which were considered nuisances and annoyances. Minor flooding, which in some cases resulted in road closures were reported. High winds caused the Route 72 Bridge to be closed for an extended period of time late in the morning on the 12th. The bridge was reopened shortly after 4:00 PM EDT on the 12th. Across the island, high winds brought down utility lines and sent debris flying. A billboard for a local ReMax real estate office on East Bay Avenue in Stafford Township was shredded on the 12th due to winds topping nearly 70 mph. In Barnegat Township (Ocean County), nearly 20 trees in various spots of town were knocked down, however there were no power outages or injuries reported. Bayshore Drive was shut down as bay water inundated it in the portion by the public beach and the municipal dock. A tree, which fell on Lower Shore Road, closed that road near the recreation complex. Bay Beach and the municipal dock parking lot were under a foot-and-a-half of water. Some of the older homes reportedly lost some roof shingles as one resident reported them laying in their yard. The strong winds even blew the shopping carts from the Genuardi's supermarket onto West Bay Avenue. ||In Surf City (Ocean County), the Causeway Bridge was closed by authorities from midday to about 5:00 PM EDT on the 12th when the winds approached 65 mph. A truck actually got stuck on the bridge as the driver could not control the drift of the vehicle. The flood-prone and low-lying Tuckerton Beach (Ocean County) experienced increased tides due to the coastal storm, which flooded some areas, and at least two cars were lost. During the morning of the 12th, residents awoke to three feet of water around their homes. There were portable toilets on their sides and floating in the middle of the street, and numerous 20-pound propane tanks also floated around. The strong winds also brought down a couple of power lines and blew a boat off of a boatlift. Some homes lost their siding while others had lost roof shingles. In Beach Haven (Ocean County), the high tide and strong onshore winds allowed water to breach over the street entrance off of Merivale and Nelsen Avenues. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably hot and humid air mass led to severe thunderstorms across northwestern New Jersey during the evening of the 10th. About 250,000 homes and businesses lost power across the northern part of the state. Power was not fully restored until the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down several trees and power lines throughout Sussex County. The worst reported damage was in and around Newton. About 5,400 homes and businesses lost power in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably hot and humid air mass helped trigger a couple of severe thunderstorms across southeast New Jersey. Lightning from one storm started a wildfire in Burlington County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down numerous trees and wires from Stafford Township north through Barnegat Township into Toms River Township. Stafford Township alone responded to twenty-six separate weather related calls. Many of the calls were about downed power lines, but there were also dozens of trees uprooted. A couple of downed trees damaged homes and vehicles. In Barnegat Township, downed trees and wires blocked roadways for up to two hours. In Toms River Township, trees and wires were also knocked down. This included the intersection of Hooper Avenue and New Jersey State Route 37. Power was restored to all by the night of the 27th, with only 66 homes and businesses still without power on the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most oppressive heat wave of the meteorological summer affected New Jersey from June 7th through the 10th. The combination of high temperatures well into the 90s to around 100 and dew point temperatures in the 70s produced apparent temperatures or heat indices values as high as around 100 in northern New Jersey and around 105 in southern New Jersey. Most of the highest temperatures occurred on the 10th, except at the shore where the highest temperatures occurred on the 9th. The highest apparent temperatures occurred on both the 7th and the 10th. ||Trying to escape the heat led to the drowning death of a 34-year-old male in the Delaware River off of Warren County on the 8th. Four men attempted to cross the Delaware River from the Hialeah Picnic Area in the Delaware Water Gap in Monroe County. They made it safely to Labar Island just south of Tocks Island. The four continued crossing the river toward New Jersey, when he started having trouble midway toward the New Jersey coast near Worthington State Forest in Hardwick Township.||The excessive heat caused many heat related injuries across the state. Many cooling centers were opened to assist senior citizens. The heat also caused a few roadways to buckle. Schools were still in session and many that did not have air conditioning dismissed children early on the 9th and 10th. A couple of schools cancelled classes altogether. Some after school activities were cancelled outright; others were shifted to air conditioned locations. The excessive heat caused some scattered and mainly localized power outages. There was about a twenty-five percent increase in vehicle distress calls. There was also a surge in purchases of air conditioners and pools.||In northwest New Jersey, 3,000 homes and businesses lost power mainly on the 9th. In Monmouth and Ocean Counties about 10,000 homes and businesses lost power. The air conditioning failed on the campus of Ocean County College, cancelling classes. A cold front helped trigger several severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon and evening of the 16th in New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down numerous trees and wires in western Morris County. In Jefferson Township, downed trees blocked the northbound lane of New Jersey State Route 15. Downed trees and wires were reported in Rockaway, Roxbury and Denville Townships. In Denville, downed trees tore down some wires. The surge damaged the electrical wiring in seven homes. A bedroom fire started in one home and four fire companies helped extinguish it. About 10,000 homes and businesses lost power in Morris County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down several power lines and trees across northeast Monmouth County. Downed trees and wires were reported in Fair Haven, Holmdel, Middletown and Neptune. About 10,000 homes and businesses lost power in the county. About 2,000 did not have power restored as of the evening of the 17th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms caused wind damage across eastern Sussex County. The majority of the severe weather occurred in the southeast part of the county. In Wantage Township, trees were knocked down on Decker Road. In Byram Township, there were six calls for downed trees. One downed tree damaged a home on Woodland Road. In Green Township, downed trees blocked two roads. In Andover Township, trees were knocked down along County Route 517. In Newton, downed wires fell onto a vehicle in a parking lot. About 5,000 homes and businesses lost power because of the damaging winds and lightning with the outages concentrated in Andover and Newton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger scattered showers and thunderstorns across New Jersey during the evening of the 14th. Because of the hot and humid air mass preceding this front, some of the storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees and wires were knocked down by a severe thunderstorm in Independence Township.

The fire was contained on the 29th. No homes or campers were threatened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most oppressive heat wave of the meteorological summer affected New Jersey from June 7th through the 10th. The combination of high temperatures well into the 90s to around 100 and dew point temperatures in the 70s produced apparent temperatures or heat indices values as high as around 100 in northern New Jersey and around 105 in southern New Jersey. Most of the highest temperatures occurred on the 10th, except at the shore where the highest temperatures occurred on the 9th. The highest apparent temperatures occurred on both the 7th and the 10th. ||Trying to escape the heat led to the drowning death of a 34-year-old male in the Delaware River off of Warren County on the 8th. Four men attempted to cross the Delaware River from the Hialeah Picnic Area in the Delaware Water Gap in Monroe County. They made it safely to Labar Island just south of Tocks Island. The four continued crossing the river toward New Jersey, when he started having trouble midway toward the New Jersey coast near Worthington State Forest in Hardwick Township.||The excessive heat caused many heat related injuries across the state. Many cooling centers were opened to assist senior citizens. The heat also caused a few roadways to buckle. Schools were still in session and many that did not have air conditioning dismissed children early on the 9th and 10th. A couple of schools cancelled classes altogether. Some after school activities were cancelled outright; others were shifted to air conditioned locations. The excessive heat caused some scattered and mainly localized power outages. There was about a twenty-five percent increase in vehicle distress calls. There was also a surge in purchases of air conditioners and pools.||In northwest New Jersey, 3,000 homes and businesses lost power mainly on the 9th. In Middlesex County, cooling centers were opened at libraries and community centers in four municipalities. In Pert Amboy, a medical tent saw a steady stream of visitors complaining of dehydration at Puerto Rican Day on the 7th. Northbound New Jersey State Route 35 was closed on the afternoon of the 8th after a drawbridge between Sayreville and Old Bridge got stuck in the upright position. In Monmouth and Ocean Counties about 10,000 homes and businesses lost power. The air conditioning failed on the campus of Ocean County College, cancelling classes. In Mercer County, schools in Trenton were closed on the 10th. Cooling centers were opened in all of the municipalities' neighborhood community centers as well as nine county libraries. In Camden (Camden County), spray pools were opened. In Atlantic County, the Dorset Avenue Bridge in Ventnor was closed. In Cape May County, on the afternoon of the 8th New Jersey State Route 52 Causeway Bridge into Ocean City was closed for 90 minutes because the bridge would not close. Boats were detoured the rest of the afternoon until temperatures dropped. Heat sensitive crops such as lettuce and zucchini wilted on some southern New Jersey farms.    ||Highest temperatures included 100 degrees in Hammonton (Atlantic County), Wrightstown (Burlington County), Berkeley Township and Toms River (both Ocean County), 99 degrees in Hillsborough (Somerset County) and Mount Laurel (Burlington County), 98 degrees in Sicklerville (Gloucester County), the Atlantic City International Airport and Margate (both Atlantic County), Millville (Cumberland County) and Lindenwold (Camden County), 97 degrees in Fort Monmouth (Monmouth County), Boonton (Morris County), Hightstown (Mercer County), New Brunswick (Middlesex County) and Upper Deerfield (Cumberland County), 96 degrees in Woodstown (Salem County), Chatham (Morris County), Cape May (Cape May County), Belvidere (Warren County), Flemington (Hunterdon County), Andover (Sussex County), Trenton (Mercer County) and Woodbine (Cape May County), 94 degrees in Hackettstown (Warren County) and 92 degrees in Barnegat (Ocean County). ||The heat wave helped June 2008 to be one of the warmest Junes on record. At the Atlantic City International Airport, the June monthly mean temperature of 74.9 degrees (5.2 degrees warmer than normal) was the warmest June on record. In Trenton, the June monthly mean temperature of 74.3 degrees (3.3 degrees warmer than normal) was the 7th warmest June on record. For the state of New Jersey, it was the 2nd warmest June on record since 1895.

A cold front helped trigger several severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon and evening of the 16th in New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail fell along Interstate 80 near exit 25 in Mount Olive Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger scattered showers and thunderstorns across New Jersey during the evening of the 14th. Because of the hot and humid air mass preceding this front, some of the storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down numerous large tree limbs and wires in Mercer County from Hopewell Township south throough Lawrence and Hamilton Townships. Additional large tree limbs and wires were knocked down in Princeton Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger scattered showers and thunderstorns across New Jersey during the evening of the 14th. Because of the hot and humid air mass preceding this front, some of the storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced wind damage along a southwest to northeast axis in Monmouth County. Wind damage was reported from Freehold Township northeast through Colts Neck Township, Middletown Township and Atlantic Highlands. The worst reported wind damage was in Middletown Township. A portion of Navasink River Road remained closed into the 15th because of downed trees. Power outages lasted into the 15th in both Middletown and Atlantic Highlands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger scattered showers and thunderstorns across New Jersey during the evening of the 14th. Because of the hot and humid air mass preceding this front, some of the storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gust front from a severe thunderstorm tore down a few trees in Jackson Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger scattered showers and thunderstorns across New Jersey during the evening of the 14th. Because of the hot and humid air mass preceding this front, some of the storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A family of four were struck and injured by lightning while carrying metal chairs and walking to their vehicle during a rain delay at a baseball game at Walters Park in Phillipsburg. All four collapsed on the field and could not get up after they were struck. They were all hospitalized. The 38-year-old father lost consciousness until the ambulance arrived. He suffered a scratched cornea and muscle soreness in his ankle and back. The 40-year-old mother suffered second degree burns on her back. Their 11-year-old son suffered a cut on his side. Their 9-year-old son also scratched his cornea and had blisters on his feet. All were released from the hospital by the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger scattered showers and thunderstorns across New Jersey during the evening of the 14th. Because of the hot and humid air mass preceding this front, some of the storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tree limb outside of a home on Castle Avenue in Jackson Township. The limb caught on fire, fell down and damaged the roof, side and mirror of a vehicle. Two primary electrical wires and service wires on the street were also knocked to the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold frontal passage helped produce thunderstorms across southern New Jersey on the evening of the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house on Oak Street in Westville. The force of the lightning strike blew outlets from the walls and knocked out air conditioners from windows. A large tree was also splintered by the lightning. A family of five was displaced, but no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain associated with Tropical Storm Hanna caused widespread heavy rain and poor drainage flooding, especially in the northern part of New Jersey. The runoff also caused some isolated flooding along some of the smaller streams and creeks in the area. Rain moved into the region around noon EDT, fell heavy at times during the late afternoon and early evening and ended during the late evening. Storm totals ranged from around 2 to around 5 inches with the highest amounts in northern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Event precipitation totals included 3.64 inches in Pennington, 3.02 inches in Trenton, 2.94 inches in Ewing and 2.67 inches in Lawrence.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Event precipitation totals included 3.67 inches in Franklin, 3.53 inches in Califon, 3.45 inches in Flemington, 3.38 inches in Annandale and 2.99 inches in Pittstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably dry August occurred across the state of New Jersey with the greatest departures from normal in the central part of the state. This exacerbated crop damage that was already inflicted by the heat and the large hail storm in the southern part of the state on August 10th.  The combination of the June heat and the August hail storm and drought led the United States Secretary of Agriculture Edward Schafer to declare ten central and southern New Jersey Counties a natural disaster on September 22nd. Mercer, Monmouth, Burlington, Ocean, Camden, Gloucester, Atlantic, Salem, Cumberland and Cape May Counties were included in the declaration.  On a county weighted average, August monthly precipitation ranged from 1.3 in Cape May County to 3.6 inches in Passaic County. Normal is around 4.3 inches. The actual August monthly precipitation total at the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County) was 2.44 inches, 1.88 inches drier than normal and at Mercer County Airport 1.24 inches, 2.56 inches drier than normal. It was the tenth driest August on record at Mercer County Airport (Trenton).

A strong onshore flow developed between a low pressure system that moved west into the Carolinas on the 25th and a high pressure system parked over New England. The strongest winds occurred on the 25th and 26th with peak gusts that reached between 40 and 47 mph from coastal Ocean County southward. The onshore flow weakened on the 27th, but swells and pounding surf from Hurricane Kyle lasted through the 28th. The combination of both caused beach erosion along the New Jersey shore.||In Monmouth and Ocean Counties, the vertical cuts on the beaches averaged two to four feet with the sloping cuts around 40 feet. In Atlantic and Cape May Counties, the vertical cuts averaged one to two feet. But in Cape May County, some dunes were cut from Strathmere south through North Wildwood. In Ocean City, an outfall and jetty were exposed. In Strathmere, 500 cubic yards were missing from the corner of one dune. One backyard bulkhead collapsed. From Winthrop Avenue to Williams Avenue, a four to five cut was made in a dune. In Avalon, dune cuts as high as six feet and as wide as twelve feet were reported at the north end of the borough. In Stone Harbor, dune cuts reached three feet high and four feet wide toward the south end of the borough. In North Wildwood, an inland beach dune had a cut that was four feet high and three feet wide. ||Peak winds included 47 mph in Barnegat (Ocean County), 44 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County) and 41 mph in Sea Girt (Monmouth County).

The runoff also caused some isolated flooding along some of the smaller streams and creeks in the area. Rain moved into the region around noon EDT, fell heavy at times during the late afternoon and early evening and ended during the late evening. Storm totals ranged from around 2 to around 5 inches with the highest amounts in northern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy rain caused flooding along Interstate 78 near Alpha Borough and the Pohatcong Township and Greenwich Township border. The eastbound lanes were closed during the evening and traffic was diverted onto County Route 173. Event precipitation totals included 3.40 inches in Hope, 2.44 inches in Stewartsville, 2.39 inches in Hackettstown and 2.03 inches in Blairstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of the heavy rain that fell with Tropical Storm Hanna on the 6th and heavy rain that fell along and around the cold frontal passage on the 9th, led to flash flooding of roadways and small streams in the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain led to minor flash flooding of streams and roadways in and around Netcong Borough. In the central part of the county, several roads were flooded in Dover. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between two and two and a half inches around the borough.

A wildfire burned 3200 acres on the Fort Dix Military Base just on the Ocean County side of the base. The fire started in a containment area of the base and was first reported on August 28th. It remained in a remote firing range area of the base. As a precaution live fire exercises were halted in the area. The fire never threatened any buildings on the base and natural fire breaks prevented it from spreading to those areas. The fire burned out at 130 p.m. EDT on September 3rd. Fort Dix firefighters as well as fire fighters from New Egypt and Cookstone battled the blaze.

A 37-year-old man died after he became trapped within a northbound rip current as the tide was receding on the 24th. This occurred on the Atlantic Avenue Beach in Spring Lake in Monmouth County. His body was found submerged near a jetty about 30 minutes after he disappeared at 330 p.m. EDT. Two other water rescues from rip currents occurred that day in the same area.

A strong onshore flow developed between a low pressure system that moved west into the Carolinas on the 25th and a high pressure system parked over New England. The strongest winds occurred on the 25th and 26th with peak gusts that reached between 40 and 47 mph from coastal Ocean County southward. The onshore flow weakened on the 27th, but swells and pounding surf from Hurricane Kyle lasted through the 28th. The combination of both caused beach erosion along the New Jersey shore.||In Monmouth and Ocean Counties, the vertical cuts on the beaches averaged two to four feet with the sloping cuts around 40 feet. In Atlantic and Cape May Counties, the vertical cuts averaged one to two feet. But in Cape May County, some dunes were cut from Strathmere south through North Wildwood. In Ocean City, an outfall and jetty were exposed. In Strathmere, 500 cubic yards were missing from the corner of one dune. One backyard bulkhead collapsed. From Winthrop Avenue to Williams Avenue, a four to five cut was made in a dune. In Avalon, dune cuts as high as six feet and as wide as twelve feet were reported at the north end of the borough. In Stone Harbor, dune cuts reached three feet high and four feet wide toward the south end of the borough. In North Wildwood, an inland beach dune had a cut that was four feet high and three feet wide. ||Peak winds included 47 mph in Barnegat (Ocean County), 44 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County) and 41 mph in Sea Girt (Monmouth County).

Heavy rain associated with Tropical Storm Hanna caused widespread heavy rain and poor drainage flooding, especially in the northern part of New Jersey. The runoff also caused some isolated flooding along some of the smaller streams and creeks in the area. Rain moved into the region around noon EDT, fell heavy at times during the late afternoon and early evening and ended during the late evening. Storm totals ranged from around 2 to around 5 inches with the highest amounts in northern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Event precipitation totals included 4.59 inches in Basking Ridge, 4.40 inches in Bridgewater, 4.33 inches in Hillsborough and 3.78 inches in Somerville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Hanna brought heavy rain and strong winds in New Jersey as well as some minor tidal flooding in Cape May County, up Delaware Bay and into the Delaware River on the 6th. Rain moved into the region around noon EDT, fell heavy at times during the late afternoon and early evening and ended during the late evening. Storm totals ranged from around 2 to around 5 inches with the highest amounts in northern New Jersey. The strongest winds occurred during the afternoon in the southern part of the state and the evening in the northern part of the state. The highest reported wind gust was 58 mph. Over 10,000 homes and businesses lost power. All power was restored by the 7th. Minor tidal flooding occurred during the afternoon and evening as the surge averaged around two feet. Many scheduled events were either cancelled or postponed.  | |The combination of the winds and heavy rain caused some weak trees and tree limbs to be knocked down throughout the state. This caused some isolated road closures in Burlington, Cumberland and Morris Counties. U.S. Route 46 was closed in Denville (Morris County) because of downed wires. Most of the other closures in the state were smaller roadways. About 2,300 homes and businesses lost power in Morris County; 2,600 homes and businesses in Monmouth and Ocean Counties and 4,700 homes and businesses in Cumberland County.||Minor tidal flooding occurred in the back bays of Ocean City and Lower Township in Cape May County. Three hundred truckloads of sand were dumped in Strathmere (Upper Township) to help combat some dune erosion. In Salem County, tidal flooding backed into two creeks. This was exacerbated in part by the freshwater run-off from the heavy rain. Tidal flooding was reported on New Jersey State Route 45 in Salem along the Fenwick Creek and on New Jersey State Route 49 in Quinton along the Alloway Creek.||Peak wind gusts included 58 mph in Barnegat (Ocean County), 51 mph in North Wildwood (Cape May County), 47 mph in Point Pleasant (Monmouth County), 45 mph in Keansburg and Ocean Grove (Monmouth County), 44 mph in Burlington Township (Burlington County), 43 mph in Atlantic City (Atlantic County) and Pittsgrove (Salem County) and 41 mph in Millville. The high tide at Reedys Point (New Castle County, Delaware) peaked at 7.22 feet above mean lower low water at 524 p.m. EDT on the 6th. Minor tidal flooding starts at 7.2 feet above mean lower low water. The high tide in Philadelphia peaked at 8.40 feet above mean lower low water at 726 p.m. EDT on the 6th. Minor tidal flooding starts at 8.2 feet above mean lower low water.||Tropical Storm Hanna made landfall at about 320 a.m. EDT on September 6th near the border of North and South Carolina. The tropical storm proceeded to move northeast. At 8 a.m. EDT, Hanna was near Goldsboro, North Carolina; at 11 a.m. EDT just southeast of Emporia, Virginia; at 2 p.m. EDT near Williamsburg, Virginia, at 5 p.m. EDT in Dorchester County, Maryland. Hanna then cut across northwest Sussex County in Delaware and entered Delaware Bay and made landfall in New Jersey close to 7 p.m. EDT in eastern Cumberland County. At 8 p.m. EDT Hanna was located in northern Atlantic County, New Jersey and exited the state near Island Beach State Park in Ocean County. At 11 p.m. EDT, Hanna was about to make landfall in western Suffolk County in New York State. Hanna continued to move northeast through eastern Long Island and New England overnight on the 6th.

Tropical Storm Hanna brought heavy rain and strong winds in New Jersey as well as some minor tidal flooding in Cape May County, up Delaware Bay and into the Delaware River on the 6th. Heavy rain associated with Tropical Storm Hanna caused widespread heavy rain and poor drainage flooding, especially in the northern part of New Jersey. The runoff also caused some isolated flooding along some of the smaller streams and creeks in the area. Rain moved into the region around noon EDT, fell heavy at times during the late afternoon and early evening and ended during the late evening. Storm totals ranged from around 2 to around 5 inches with the highest amounts in northern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Event precipitation totals included 3.90 inches in Absecon, 3.31 inches in Margate, 2.89 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport and 1.69 inches in Folsom.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably dry August occurred across the state of New Jersey with the greatest departures from normal in the central part of the state. This exacerbated crop damage that was already inflicted by the heat and the large hail storm in the southern part of the state on August 10th.  The combination of the June heat and the August hail storm and drought led the United States Secretary of Agriculture Edward Schafer to declare ten central and southern New Jersey Counties a natural disaster on September 22nd. Mercer, Monmouth, Burlington, Ocean, Camden, Gloucester, Atlantic, Salem, Cumberland and Cape May Counties were included in the declaration.  On a county weighted average, August monthly precipitation ranged from 1.3 in Cape May County to 3.6 inches in Passaic County. Normal is around 4.3 inches. The actual August monthly precipitation total at the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County) was 2.44 inches, 1.88 inches drier than normal and at Mercer County Airport 1.24 inches, 2.56 inches drier than normal. It was the tenth driest August on record at Mercer County Airport (Trenton).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain led to flooding and the closure of several major roads in Middlesex County. In New Brusnwick and East Brunswick, Ryders Lane was closed. The northbound lanes of New Jersey State Route 18 were closed in New Brunswick. In Woodbridge, U.S. Route 9, New Jersey State Route 27 and Leesville Avenue were all closed into the evening. Event precipitation totals included 5.00 inches in East Brunswick, 4.80 inches in Metuchen, 4.50 inches in Old Bridge and 4.09 inches in New Brunswick.

The Tacony-Palmyra Bridge Toll Plaza was flooded and all eastbound traffic into New Jersey was halted for about 45 minutes until the water receded. Two other roadways in Palmyra were also closed. Doppler Radar adjusted storm total amounts exceeded three inches in the borough.

Tropical Storm Hanna brought heavy rain and strong winds in New Jersey as well as some minor tidal flooding in Cape May County, up Delaware Bay and into the Delaware River on the 6th. Rain moved into the region around noon EDT, fell heavy at times during the late afternoon and early evening and ended during the late evening. Storm totals ranged from around 2 to around 5 inches with the highest amounts in northern New Jersey. The strongest winds occurred during the afternoon in the southern part of the state and the evening in the northern part of the state. The highest reported wind gust was 58 mph. Over 10,000 homes and businesses lost power. All power was restored by the 7th. Minor tidal flooding occurred during the afternoon and evening as the surge averaged around two feet. Many scheduled events were either cancelled or postponed.  | |The combination of the winds and heavy rain caused some weak trees and tree limbs to be knocked down throughout the state. This caused some isolated road closures in Burlington, Cumberland and Morris Counties. U.S. Route 46 was closed in Denville (Morris County) because of downed wires. Most of the other closures in the state were smaller roadways. About 2,300 homes and businesses lost power in Morris County; 2,600 homes and businesses in Monmouth and Ocean Counties and 4,700 homes and businesses in Cumberland County.||Minor tidal flooding occurred in the back bays of Ocean City and Lower Township in Cape May County. Three hundred truckloads of sand were dumped in Strathmere (Upper Township) to help combat some dune erosion. In Salem County, tidal flooding backed into two creeks. An unseasonably dry August occurred across the state of New Jersey with the greatest departures from normal in the central part of the state. This exacerbated crop damage that was already inflicted by the heat and the large hail storm in the southern part of the state on August 10th.  The combination of the June heat and the August hail storm and drought led the United States Secretary of Agriculture Edward Schafer to declare ten central and southern New Jersey Counties a natural disaster on September 22nd. Mercer, Monmouth, Burlington, Ocean, Camden, Gloucester, Atlantic, Salem, Cumberland and Cape May Counties were included in the declaration.  On a county weighted average, August monthly precipitation ranged from 1.3 in Cape May County to 3.6 inches in Passaic County. Normal is around 4.3 inches. The actual August monthly precipitation total at the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County) was 2.44 inches, 1.88 inches drier than normal and at Mercer County Airport 1.24 inches, 2.56 inches drier than normal. It was the tenth driest August on record at Mercer County Airport (Trenton).

A strong onshore flow developed between a low pressure system that moved west into the Carolinas on the 25th and a high pressure system parked over New England. The strongest winds occurred on the 25th and 26th with peak gusts that reached between 40 and 47 mph from coastal Ocean County southward. The onshore flow weakened on the 27th, but swells and pounding surf from Hurricane Kyle lasted through the 28th. The combination of both caused beach erosion along the New Jersey shore.||In Monmouth and Ocean Counties, the vertical cuts on the beaches averaged two to four feet with the sloping cuts around 40 feet. In Atlantic and Cape May Counties, the vertical cuts averaged one to two feet. But in Cape May County, some dunes were cut from Strathmere south through North Wildwood. In Ocean City, an outfall and jetty were exposed. In Strathmere, 500 cubic yards were missing from the corner of one dune. One backyard bulkhead collapsed. From Winthrop Avenue to Williams Avenue, a four to five cut was made in a dune. In Avalon, dune cuts as high as six feet and as wide as twelve feet were reported at the north end of the borough. In Stone Harbor, dune cuts reached three feet high and four feet wide toward the south end of the borough. In North Wildwood, an inland beach dune had a cut that was four feet high and three feet wide. ||Peak winds included 47 mph in Barnegat (Ocean County), 44 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County) and 41 mph in Sea Girt (Monmouth County).

Heavy rain associated with Tropical Storm Hanna caused widespread heavy rain and poor drainage flooding, especially in the northern part of New Jersey. The runoff also caused some isolated flooding along some of the smaller streams and creeks in the area. Rain moved into the region around noon EDT, fell heavy at times during the late afternoon and early evening and ended during the late evening. Storm totals ranged from around 2 to around 5 inches with the highest amounts in northern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the heavy rain caused flooding along the Whippany Creek within Morristown. The Whippany fire house on New Jersey State Route 10 was sandbagged to prevent flooding from the creek from reaching it in Hanover Township. Flooding was also reported along a small tributary to the creek along Horsehill Road in Hanover Township. The Whippany Creek in Morristown was above its 6 foot flood stage from 614 p.m. EDT through 1022 p.m. EDT on the 6th. It crested at 6.38 feet at 8 p.m. EDT. Precipitation storm totals in Morris County included 6.00 in Morris Plains, 4.72 inches in Parsippany, 4.59 inches in Randolph, 3.84 inches in Roxbury and 3.70 inches in Boonton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably dry August occurred across the state of New Jersey with the greatest departures from normal in the central part of the state. This exacerbated crop damage that was already inflicted by the heat and the large hail storm in the southern part of the state on August 10th.  The combination of the June heat and the August hail storm and drought led the United States Secretary of Agriculture Edward Schafer to declare ten central and southern New Jersey Counties a natural disaster on September 22nd. Mercer, Monmouth, Burlington, Ocean, Camden, Gloucester, Atlantic, Salem, Cumberland and Cape May Counties were included in the declaration.  On a county weighted average, August monthly precipitation ranged from 1.3 in Cape May County to 3.6 inches in Passaic County. Normal is around 4.3 inches. The actual August monthly precipitation total at the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County) was 2.44 inches, 1.88 inches drier than normal and at Mercer County Airport 1.24 inches, 2.56 inches drier than normal. It was the tenth driest August on record at Mercer County Airport (Trenton).

Tropical Storm Hanna brought heavy rain and strong winds in New Jersey as well as some minor tidal flooding in Cape May County, up Delaware Bay and into the Delaware River on the 6th. Rain moved into the region around noon EDT, fell heavy at times during the late afternoon and early evening and ended during the late evening. Storm totals ranged from around 2 to around 5 inches with the highest amounts in northern New Jersey. The strongest winds occurred during the afternoon in the southern part of the state and the evening in the northern part of the state. The highest reported wind gust was 58 mph. Over 10,000 homes and businesses lost power. All power was restored by the 7th. Minor tidal flooding occurred during the afternoon and evening as the surge averaged around two feet. Many scheduled events were either cancelled or postponed.  | |The combination of the winds and heavy rain caused some weak trees and tree limbs to be knocked down throughout the state. This caused some isolated road closures in Burlington, Cumberland and Morris Counties. U.S. Route 46 was closed in Denville (Morris County) because of downed wires. Most of the other closures in the state were smaller roadways. About 2,300 homes and businesses lost power in Morris County; 2,600 homes and businesses in Monmouth and Ocean Counties and 4,700 homes and businesses in Cumberland County.||Minor tidal flooding occurred in the back bays of Ocean City and Lower Township in Cape May County. Three hundred truckloads of sand were dumped in Strathmere (Upper Township) to help combat some dune erosion. In Salem County, tidal flooding backed into two creeks. This was exacerbated in part by the freshwater run-off from the heavy rain. Tidal flooding was reported on New Jersey State Route 45 in Salem along the Fenwick Creek and on New Jersey State Route 49 in Quinton along the Alloway Creek.||Peak wind gusts included 58 mph in Barnegat (Ocean County), 51 mph in North Wildwood (Cape May County), 47 mph in Point Pleasant (Monmouth County), 45 mph in Keansburg and Ocean Grove (Monmouth County), 44 mph in Burlington Township (Burlington County), 43 mph in Atlantic City (Atlantic County) and Pittsgrove (Salem County) and 41 mph in Millville. The high tide at Reedys Point (New Castle County, Delaware) peaked at 7.22 feet above mean lower low water at 524 p.m. EDT on the 6th. Minor tidal flooding starts at 7.2 feet above mean lower low water. The high tide in Philadelphia peaked at 8.40 feet above mean lower low water at 726 p.m. EDT on the 6th. Minor tidal flooding starts at 8.2 feet above mean lower low water.||Tropical Storm Hanna made landfall at about 320 a.m. EDT on September 6th near the border of North and South Carolina. The tropical storm proceeded to move northeast. At 8 a.m. EDT, Hanna was near Goldsboro, North Carolina; at 11 a.m. EDT just southeast of Emporia, Virginia; at 2 p.m. EDT near Williamsburg, Virginia, at 5 p.m. EDT in Dorchester County, Maryland. Hanna then cut across northwest Sussex County in Delaware and entered Delaware Bay and made landfall in New Jersey close to 7 p.m. EDT in eastern Cumberland County. At 8 p.m. EDT Hanna was located in northern Atlantic County, New Jersey and exited the state near Island Beach State Park in Ocean County. At 11 p.m. EDT, Hanna was about to make landfall in western Suffolk County in New York State. Hanna continued to move northeast through eastern Long Island and New England overnight on the 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually intense upper level low for August helped trigger thunderstorms in New Jersey. While the hail reports did not reach severe criteria, small hail was reported with the thunderstorms in Mercer, Middlesex and Warren Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down large tree limbs in Robbinsville Township. Small hail was also reported with the thunderstorm in Hamilton and Princeton Townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong for August upper air disturbance helped trigger numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across New Jersey. In addition these storms moved over the same areas of Salem and Gloucester Counties and produced flash flooding of streams and fields.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused flooding of small creeks and roadways in Pitman Borough. The worst reported flooding was near and along the railroad line where vehicles became stuck and water rescues were performed. No serious injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rip currents caused numerous water rescues along Monmouth County beaches on September 2nd. Nearly forty people were rescued from rip currents in Bradley Beach and Belmar. In Bradley Beach, nine people, including six children between the ages of ten and sixteen, were rescued near the LaRaine Avenue Beach. Six people were hospitalized, treated and released.
A secondary cold frontal passage coupled with an upper air disturbance aloft helped trigger a severe thunderstorm in northern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to nickel size hail fell in Kinnelon. The same thunderstorm dropped pea size hail in Butler.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, just ahead of an advancing warm front, resulted in a microburst in Bergen County during the morning of the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst occurred in Harrington Park along a west to east running path one-quarter mile long and 200 yards wide. This path was roughly from 21 Harriot Avenue, where it appears the microburst made first contact with the ground, to the western border of the town. The microburst brought down multiple large tree branches, and a few large trees, causing damage to 5 to 10 houses and some parked cars, including a 2008 limited edition Mustang Bullitt. Based on the damage, peak winds from this microburst were estimated at around 90 mph.  In addition, multiple trees were downed in Old Tappan near where the Hackensack River goes through the town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomolous slow moving upper level low pressure system in combination with a weak surface cold front and local seabreezes triggered thunderstorms producing torrential rainfall which led to flash flooding in Bergen County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of Molnar Dr. and Midland Ave. in Elmwood Park was impassable due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomolous low pressure system over southeast New York produced numerous thunderstorms with torrential rainfall which led to flash flooding in northeast New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North Avenue in Elizabeth was closed due to flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Elmora Ave. at Fay Ave. in Elizabeth was closed due to flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maple Street in Fort Lee was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front located in central Pennsylvania around sunrise on the 2nd helped trigger strong to severe thunderstorms over New Jersey through the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees and tree limbs in Villas (Lower Township).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front located in central Pennsylvania around sunrise on the 2nd helped trigger strong to severe thunderstorms over New Jersey through the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down several trees and wires in Middle and Dennis Townships across central Cape May County. Downed trees were reported along New Jersey State Route 47, Court House and Dennisville Roads. A 54 mph wind gust was also measured at the Cape May Coast Guard Station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front located in central Pennsylvania around sunrise on the 2nd helped trigger strong to severe thunderstorms over New Jersey through the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down a large tree and several large tree limbs in Hopewell Township.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 17 southbound was closed at Paterson Ave. in East Rutherford and near Highland Cross in Rutherford due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 19-year-old man drowned off an unguarded portion on the northern part of  Island Beach State Park in Ocean County on the morning of August 1st. His 57-year-old father was unable to rescue him. His father had to be hospitalized after barely making it out of the water. About 75 people were involved in rescue efforts.
An anomolous low pressure system over southeast New York produced numerous thunderstorms with torrential rainfall which led to flash flooding in northeast New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three feet of water inundated Frelinghuysen Ave. in Newark.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong for August upper air disturbance helped trigger numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across New Jersey. Severe thunderstorms started developing along the warm front in the morning and continued through the afternoon and into the evening until a cold front moved through the state.  ||The large hail storms that occurred contributed to the United States Secretary of Agriculture Edward Schafer declaring ten central and southern New Jersey Counties a natural disaster on September 22nd. Mercer, Monmouth, Burlington, Ocean, Camden, Gloucester, Atlantic, Salem, Cumberland and Cape May Counties were included in the declaration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms dropped marble to quarter size hail across central Gloucester County from South Harrison Township east through Harrison Township, Mantua Township, Pitman Borough and Washington Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong for August upper air disturbance helped trigger numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across New Jersey. Severe thunderstorms started developing along the warm front in the morning and continued through the afternoon and into the evening until a cold front moved through the state.  ||The large hail storms that occurred contributed to the United States Secretary of Agriculture Edward Schafer declaring ten central and southern New Jersey Counties a natural disaster on September 22nd. Mercer, Monmouth, Burlington, Ocean, Camden, Gloucester, Atlantic, Salem, Cumberland and Cape May Counties were included in the declaration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail as large as golf balls caused vehicular and crop damage from Pennsville and Carney Townships eastward across mouch of the northern half of Salem County. Large amounts of hail damage occurred to farms in Mannington, Carney Point, Pilesgrove and Oldmans Township. A farm in Pedericktown (Oldman Township) saw its entire tomato, pepper, and string bean crop destroyed. Ninety percent of the crops on a 650 acre farm in Mannington Township was destroyed by hail. Many zucchini crops within the county were damaged. In addition to crop damage, vehicles were damaged by large hail. Over 100 vehicles were damaged at dealerships in Penns Grove and Carneys Point. It was estimated that it would take until mid September for auto body shops to repair all the vehicles damaged by the large hail in the county.

Severe thunderstorms started developing along the warm front in the morning and continued through the afternoon and into the evening until a cold front moved through the state.  ||The large hail storms that occurred contributed to the United States Secretary of Agriculture Edward Schafer declaring ten central and southern New Jersey Counties a natural disaster on September 22nd. Mercer, Monmouth, Burlington, Ocean, Camden, Gloucester, Atlantic, Salem, Cumberland and Cape May Counties were included in the declaration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed several trees along the 200 block of Rosenhayn Avenue in Upper Deerfield Township. The downed trees damaged four homes on the block. The left side of one home was destroyed. Another home was impaled twice by a downed tree. Another home had its awning torn away from its porch, outdoor furniture damaged and a tree on its pool. Elsewhere in Upper Deerfield Township, downed trees blocked Park Avenue. Several pine and oak trees were split and twisted. About 5,200 homes and businesses lost power in the county. Outages lasted into the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the area producing thunderstorms with torrential downpours which caused isolated flash flooding in Bergen County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New Bridge Rd., the south end of River Rd., the south end of Boulevard and Reichelt Roads; all in New Milford, were closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front located in central Pennsylvania around sunrise on the 2nd helped trigger strong to severe thunderstorms over New Jersey through the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down a few large tree limbs and a large tree in Montgomery County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front located in central Pennsylvania around sunrise on the 2nd helped trigger strong to severe thunderstorms over New Jersey through the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down a traffic light and wires in Ocean Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front located in central Pennsylvania around sunrise on the 2nd helped trigger strong to severe thunderstorms over New Jersey through the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down a few trees from Marlboro Township east through Holmdel and Middletown Townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave of low pressure moving along a stationary front draped across the local area produced thunderstorms with torrential downpours leading to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 17 northbound was impassable for 0.8 mile at the exit for Linwood Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front located in central Pennsylvania around sunrise on the 2nd helped trigger strong to severe thunderstorms over New Jersey through the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down a few trees in Holland Township and Milford Borough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front located in central Pennsylvania around sunrise on the 2nd helped trigger strong to severe thunderstorms over New Jersey through the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail fell at the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Westampton, New Jersey. Pea size hail fell from the same thunderstorm in Willingboro Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong for August upper air disturbance helped trigger numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across New Jersey. In addition these storms moved over the same areas of Salem and Gloucester Counties and produced flash flooding of streams and fields.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moving over the same areas of Woolwich Township caused flooding of fields and small streams. One farm had 35 acres of sweet potato and butternut squash under water. Adjusted for hail Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 3 and 4 inches in the township. In neighboring Harrison Township, a storm total of 3.27 inches was measured in Mullica Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some isolated pulse type severe thunderstorms occurred in northern New Jersey during the afternoon of the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down large tree limbs and wires as well as a few trees in Middlesex County from Old Bridge to Sayresville. Numerous cloud-to-ground lightning strikes also occurred with the thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong for August upper air disturbance helped trigger numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across New Jersey. Severe thunderstorms started developing along the warm front in the morning and continued through the afternoon and into the evening until a cold front moved through the state.  ||The large hail storms that occurred contributed to the United States Secretary of Agriculture Edward Schafer declaring ten central and southern New Jersey Counties a natural disaster on September 22nd. Mercer, Monmouth, Burlington, Ocean, Camden, Gloucester, Atlantic, Salem, Cumberland and Cape May Counties were included in the declaration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The last severe thunderstorm of the day proved to be the most damaging  as large hail fell for the longest period of time. Areas of large hail occurred from Harrison Township southeast through Monroe Township. Ten peach orchards in Gloucester County were damaged by the large hail. About 40 percent of the peach crop was already harvested prior to the severe thunderstorms. The fourteen peach orchards affected by repeated  severe thunderstorms with large hail in Gloucester, Camden and Atlantic Counties normally harvest about 30 million pounds of peaches.

About three peach orchards in Camden County were damaged by the large hail. About 40 percent of the peach crop was already harvested prior to the severe thunderstorms. The fourteen peach orchards affected by repeated severe thunderstorms with large hail in Gloucester, Camden and Atlantic Counties normally harvest about 30 million pounds of peaches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong for August upper air disturbance helped trigger numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across New Jersey. In addition these storms moved over the same areas of Salem and Gloucester Counties and produced flash flooding of streams and fields.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moving across the same areas caused flash flooding in Mannington, Carney's Point and Pennsville Townships. In Mannington Township, flooding washed away a small bridge over a tributary creek to the Salem River. U.S. Route 40 was flooded along the Pennsville and Carney's Point Township line. In Carney Point Township, the YMCA was partially closed after flood waters reached the fitness center, locker rooms and pool. Dunn's Park was also flooded. In Pennsville Township, flooding reached into a few homes. Adjusted for hail contamination, Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 3 and 4 inches in the area. A rainfall total of 5 inches was estimated in Mannington Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong for August upper air disturbance helped trigger numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across New Jersey. Severe thunderstorms started developing along the warm front in the morning and continued through the afternoon and into the evening until a cold front moved through the state.  ||The large hail storms that occurred contributed to the United States Secretary of Agriculture Edward Schafer declaring ten central and southern New Jersey Counties a natural disaster on September 22nd. Mercer, Monmouth, Burlington, Ocean, Camden, Gloucester, Atlantic, Salem, Cumberland and Cape May Counties were included in the declaration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the third time in the same day, a severe thunderstorm dropped large hail in Hammonton Township. Hail reached the size of ping pong balls during this severe thunderstorm. The combination of the three severe hail thunderstorms caused severe damage to crops on a few hundred acres within the township. Damage occurred on a couple mile wide corridor on either side of U.S. Route 206. Squash, pumpkin, corn, Asian greens, cucurbits and sweet potatoes were damaged. The sweet potato crop partially recovered. Also damaged were late blueberries, a few peaches and several plantings of wine grapes. Hail damage in neighboring Mullica Township was limited to some late season blueberries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold frontal passage triggered severe thunderstorms across New Jersey on the afternoon of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down trees and wires in extreme northeast Middlesex County in Woodbridge and Pert Amboy. In the Port Reading section of Woodbridge. dozens of trees were knocked down. Two trees fell on homes, one damaged an automobile. A patio umbrella was tossed about 30 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold frontal passage triggered severe thunderstorms across New Jersey on the afternoon of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm partially tore off the roof of the EWA Model Car Center on U.S. Route 22 in Green Brook Township. The roof was partially hanging off the east side of the building. The debris and rain damaged the interior of the building. Some of the ceiling collapsed. The same severe thunderstorm tore down a few trees in North Plainfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The longest heat wave of the summer affected New Jersey from July 16th through the 22nd. Some places in southern New Jersey also reached into the lower 90s on the 23rd. The heat wave ended in about the northern two thirds of the state on the 22nd as thunderstorms kept temperatures below 90 degrees and in the rest of the state on the 23rd after a cold front moved through the following morning. High temperatures were mainly in the lower to mid 90s throughout the heat wave. The hottest days occurred on the 18th, 19th or 20th. The combination of the temperatures and dew points produced apparent temperatures or heat indices of around 100F.||A 14-year-old girl drowned in an off-limit lake that used to be a quarry in Gloucester Township (Camden County) on the 18th. She was in the water with three relatives when she disappeared. ||Highest temperatures included 98 degrees in Hillsborough (Somerset County) and Berkeley Township (Ocean County), 97 degrees in Sicklerville (Gloucester County), New Brunswick (Middlesex County), Millville (Cumberland County), Trenton (Mercer County) and Mount Holly (Burlington County), 96 degrees in Cherry Hill (Camden County) and at the Atlantic City International Airport, 95 degrees in Woodstown (Salem County), 94 degrees in Pequest (Warren County), Flemington (Hunterdon County), Boonton (Morris County) and Woodbine (Cape May County) and 93 degrees in Kingwood (Hunterdon County) and Sussex (Sussex County).

An approaching upper level storm triggered numerous showers and thunderstorms across the Tri-State Region. The area was in the warm sector of the surface reflection, with the synoptic cold front to the west and warm front across northern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was downed by lightning on Morris Avenue. This tree was reported to have covered the road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed, causing a structure fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong onshore flow developed between a low pressure system that moved west into the Carolinas on the 25th and a high pressure system parked over New England. The strongest winds occurred on the 25th and 26th with peak gusts that reached between 40 and 47 mph from coastal Ocean County southward. The onshore flow weakened on the 27th, but swells and pounding surf from Hurricane Kyle lasted through the 28th. The combination of both caused beach erosion along the New Jersey shore.||In Monmouth and Ocean Counties, the vertical cuts on the beaches averaged two to four feet with the sloping cuts around 40 feet. In Atlantic and Cape May Counties, the vertical cuts averaged one to two feet. But in Cape May County, some dunes were cut from Strathmere south through North Wildwood. In Ocean City, an outfall and jetty were exposed. In Strathmere, 500 cubic yards were missing from the corner of one dune. One backyard bulkhead collapsed. From Winthrop Avenue to Williams Avenue, a four to five cut was made in a dune. In Avalon, dune cuts as high as six feet and as wide as twelve feet were reported at the north end of the borough. In Stone Harbor, dune cuts reached three feet high and four feet wide toward the south end of the borough. In North Wildwood, an inland beach dune had a cut that was four feet high and three feet wide. ||Peak winds included 47 mph in Barnegat (Ocean County), 44 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County) and 41 mph in Sea Girt (Monmouth County).

An approaching cold front that ended a week long heat wave also triggered severe thunderstorms across New Jersey during the afternoon and early evening on the 23rd. A power outage caused significant delays and over a one hour shut down (from 430 p.m. EDT until 545 p.m. EDT) of Amtrak train service between Philadelphia and Boston. These outages extended to New Jersey Transit Trains on the Northeast Corridor, North Jersey Shore Line and Midtown Direct Line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to the large hail, the same severe thunderstorm tore down several trees and wires across the northern tier of Mercer County from Hopewell Township east through Princeton Township. One street was closed in Princeton because of a downed tree.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down several trees and power lines in Pennsville and Carneys Point Townships.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm caused tree damage in Neptune Township. Severe thunderstorms throughout the afternoon combined to cause about 12,000 homes and businesses to lose power in Middlesex and Monmouth Counties.

Several waves of low pressure rode along a slow moving cold front approaching the area on July 23. A tropical airmass was already in place across the local area from Tropical Storm Cristobal which passed by to our east the previous day. The combination of these systems interacting with this airmass led to torrential rainfall and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many streets were flooded in Fort Lee.  Some of these streets were impassable.

Showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain dropped between 1.5 and 3.0 inches of rain across northwest New Jersey. The heaviest rain fell during the second half of the afternoon into the evening of the 25th. The rain caused some urban poor drainage flooding, but the recent dry weather kept streams within their banks. Rainfall totals included 2.93 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 2.16 inches in Hardyston (Sussex County), 2.08 inches in Roxbury (Morris County), 2.03 inches in Liberty (Warren County), 1.74 inches in Independence (Warren County) and 1.61 inches in Blairstown (Warren County).

The Sauder Ditch Wildfire, consumed about 1950 acres of forest before it was contained. The fire began in a secluded section of Wharton State Forest in Waterford Township (Camden County) close to 3 p.m. EDT on the 21st. This location is west of U.S. Route 206 and south of the Atsion Recreational Area. It spread into parts of Shamong Township (Burlington County) and Hammonton Township (Atlantic County). ||Gusty northwest winds along with recent dry weather helped spread the fire quickly and hampered fire fighting efforts on the 21st and 22nd. A state of emergency was declared in Shamong Township (Burlington County) on the 21st and lifted on the 22nd. About four homes and two businesses on U.S. Route 206 in Hammonton (Atlantic County) were evacuated because of fear of smoke and the fire itself on the 21st. They were allowed to return at Noon EDT on the 22nd. ||The fire reached up to 100 feet in the air and was visible from Atlantic City. Smoke was smelled as far away as Ocean County. The heavily traveled U.S. Route 206 was closed between the White Horse Pike (U.S. Route 30) in Hammonton and Atsion Lake (Shamong Township) after the fire jumped the roadway on the evening of the 21st. The roadway had sporadic closures, especially at night when the smoke became thicker, through the 25th.||About 200 fire fighters battled the blaze and used brush trucks, helicopters, bull dozers and air tankers in their efforts. Water was retreived from nearby Atsion Lake. The fire was considered twenty percent contained on the 21st, forty percent contained on the 22nd, fifty percent contained on the 23rd, seventy percent contained on the 24th, ninety percent contained on the 26th and fully contained on the morning of the 27th. A firefighter suffered an irregular heart beat from battling the blaze and one traffic cop was struck by a vehicle. ||On the morning of the 24th, an atmospheric inversion caused by a nearby high pressure system trapped the smoke near the ground. This caused thick smoke with near zero visibilities to affect Hammonton (Atlantic County) and Winslow (Camden County). All Hammonton schools were closed and the White Horse Pike (U.S. Route 30) in Hammonton and Winslow was closed. The New Jersey Motor Vehicle inspection center was closed in Winslow. Several asthmatics were treated at Cooper Hospital.||Heavy rain on the 25th helped fire fighting efforts. The peak wind gusts at Atlantic City International Airport were 37 mph on the 21st and 35 mph on the 22nd. At Atlantic City International Airport (Prior to the 25th when 1.13 inches of rain fell.) only 0.16 of an inch of rain had  fallen during the month of October.
